title: Monarchy of Canada

infobox.value: [William, Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales")[[1]](#cite_note-1)

infobox.key: [Government](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") ([structure](/wiki/Structure_of_the_Canadian_federal_government "Structure of the Canadian federal government"))
infobox.value: | [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")  * Monarch ([list](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_monarchs "List of Canadian monarchs")): [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")   + [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") ([list](/wiki/List_of_governors_general_of_Canada "List of governors general of Canada")): [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon")   + [Monarchy in the provinces](/wiki/Monarchy_in_the_Canadian_provinces "Monarchy in the Canadian provinces")     - [Lieutenant governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)") * [Royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative#Canada "Royal prerogative")

is [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")'s [form of government](/wiki/Government#Forms "Government") embodied by the Canadian [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). It is one of the key components of [Canadian sovereignty](/wiki/Canadian_sovereignty "Canadian sovereignty") and sits at the core of [Canada's constitutional federal](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism") structure and [Westminster-style](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The monarchy is the foundation of the [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") ([King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council")), [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") ([King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament")), and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial "Judicial") ([King-on-the-Bench](/wiki/Court_system_of_Canada "Court system of Canada")) branches of both [federal](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") and [provincial](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada#Government "Provinces and territories of Canada") jurisdictions.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7)[[8]](#cite_note-DCH20094-8) The current monarch is King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles

The **monarchy of Canada** is [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")'s [form of government](/wiki/Government#Forms "Government") embodied by the Canadian [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). It is one of the key components of [Canadian sovereignty](/wiki/Canadian_sovereignty "Canadian sovereignty") and sits at the core of [Canada's constitutional federal](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism") structure and [Westminster-style](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The monarchy is the foundation of the [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") ([King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council")), [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") ([King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament")), and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial "Judicial") ([King-on-the-Bench](/wiki/Court_system_of_Canada "Court system of Canada")) branches of both [federal](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") and [provincial](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada#Government "Provinces and territories of Canada") jurisdictions.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7)[[8]](#cite_note-DCH20094-8) The current monarch is King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), who has reigned since 8 September 2022.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Although the sovereign is [shared](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with [14 other independent countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") within the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), each country's monarchy is separate and legally distinct.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-evolve-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) As a result, the current monarch is officially titled [*King of Canada*](/wiki/Title_and_style_of_the_Canadian_monarch "Title and style of the Canadian monarch") and, in this capacity, he and other members of the royal family undertake public and private functions domestically and abroad as representatives of Canada. However, the monarch is the only member of the royal family with any [constitutional](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") role.

The monarch lives in the United Kingdom, and almost all of the royal governmental and ceremonial duties in Canada are carried out by the monarch's representative, the [governor general of Canada](/wiki/Governor_general_of_Canada "Governor general of Canada") (the main exception being the appointment of the governor general themself, and the [extraordinary appointment of extra senators](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada#Extraordinary_appointment_of_additional_senators "Senate of Canada")).[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) In each of [Canada's provinces](/wiki/Canada%27s_provinces "Canada's provinces"), [the monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_in_the_Canadian_provinces "Monarchy in the Canadian provinces") is represented by a [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant Governor (Canada)"). As territories fall under the federal jurisdiction, they each have a commissioner, rather than a lieutenant governor, who represents the federal [Crown-in-Council](/wiki/Governor_in_Council "Governor in Council") directly.

All executive authority is vested in the sovereign, so the monarch's consent is necessary for [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") and [orders-in-council](/wiki/Orders-in-council "Orders-in-council") to have legal effect. As well, the monarch is part of the Parliament of Canada, so [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") is required to allow for [bills](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") to become law. While the power for these acts stems from the Canadian people through the constitutional [conventions](/wiki/Convention_(norm)#Government "Convention (norm)") of democracy,[[24]](#cite_note-24) executive authority remains vested in [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") and is only entrusted by the sovereign to the government on behalf of the people. This underlines the Crown's role in safeguarding the rights, freedoms, and democratic system of government of Canadians, reinforcing the fact that "governments are the servants of the people and not the reverse".[[25]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Forseyp1-26) Thus, within Canada's [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") the sovereign's direct participation in any of these areas of governance is normally limited, with the sovereign typically exercising executive authority only with the [advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent") of the [Cabinet of Canada](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada"), and the sovereign's legislative and judicial responsibilities largely carried out through the [Parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") as well as judges and [justices of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace#Canada "Justice of the peace").[[25]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-25) There are, though, cases where the sovereign or their representative would have a duty to act directly and independently under the [doctrine of necessity](/wiki/Doctrine_of_necessity "Doctrine of necessity") to prevent genuinely unconstitutional acts.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) In these respects, the sovereign and his viceroys are custodians of the Crown's [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power#Canada "Reserve power") and represent the "power of the people above government and political parties".[[29]](#cite_note-SKEX10-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Put another way, the Crown functions as the guarantor of Canada's continuous and stable [governance](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") and as a [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisanism "Nonpartisanism") safeguard against the [abuse of power](/wiki/Abuse_of_power "Abuse of power").[[25]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-25)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Canada has been described as "one of the oldest continuing monarchies in the world" of today.[[16]](#cite_note-evolve-16)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Parts of what is now Canada have been under a monarchy since as early as the 15th century as a result of colonial settlement and often competing claims made on territory in the name of the English (and later British) and French crowns.[[n 1]](#cite_note-44) Monarchical government has developed as the result of [colonization](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") by the [French colonial empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire") and [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") competing for territory in North America and a corresponding [succession](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_monarchs "List of Canadian monarchs") of French and British sovereigns reigning over [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") and [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), respectively. As a result of the [conquest of New France](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_France "Conquest of New France"), claims by French monarchs were extinguished and what became [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") came under the hegemony of the British monarchy which ultimately evolved into the Canadian monarchy of today.[[35]](#cite_note-Harper-35)[[39]](#cite_note-Kenney-39)[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40)[[42]](#cite_note-Senate-42)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-Monet-46) With the exception of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") from [1649 to 1660](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"), no part of what is now Canada has been a republic or part of a republic;[[46]](#cite_note-47) though, there have been isolated calls for the country to become one. The Crown, however, is considered to be "entrenched" into the governmental framework.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) The institution that is Canada's system of constitutional monarchy is sometimes [colloquially](/wiki/Colloquially "Colloquially") referred to as the *[Maple](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") Crown*[[n 2]](#cite_note-Maple-53) or *Crown of Maples*,[[52]](#cite_note-54) Canada having developed a "recognizably Canadian brand of monarchy".[[53]](#cite_note-Jackson14-55)

## International and domestic aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: International and domestic aspects")]

Further information: [Commonwealth realm § Relationship of the realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm#Relationship_of_the_realms "Commonwealth realm")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Commonwealth_realm_map.svg/250px-Commonwealth_realm_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_realm_map.svg)

Commonwealth realms

Overseas territories of Commonwealth realms

---

 The 15 realms of which King Charles III is the reigning sovereign

The monarch is shared in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with 14 other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") within the 56-member [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). As he resides[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-58)[[57]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-59) in the United Kingdom, [viceroys](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") (the [governor general of Canada](/wiki/Governor_general_of_Canada "Governor general of Canada") in the federal sphere and a [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant governor (Canada)") in each province) represent the sovereign in Canada and are able to carry out most of the royal governmental duties, even when the monarch is in the country[[n 3]](#cite_note-ViceDuties-61) Nevertheless, the monarch can carry out Canadian constitutional and ceremonial duties abroad.[[n 4]](#cite_note-RoyVis-62)[[n 5]](#cite_note-RoyDuties-66)

The evolution of the role of the governor general from being both a representative of the sovereign and an "agent of the British government" who " in matters deemed to be of 'imperial' concern... acted on the instructions of the British [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office")"[[62]](#cite_note-67) to being solely a representative of the monarch developed with a rise in [Canadian nationalism](/wiki/Canadian_nationalism "Canadian nationalism") following the end of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") culminating in the passage of the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") in 1931.[[63]](#cite_note-MacLeod9-68)[[64]](#cite_note-69) Since then, the Crown has had both a shared and a separate character: the sovereign's role as monarch of Canada has been distinct from their position as monarch of any other realm,[[n 4]](#cite_note-RoyVis-62)[[16]](#cite_note-evolve-16)[[65]](#cite_note-Trepanier28-70)[[66]](#cite_note-Torrance34-71)[[67]](#cite_note-OCCanThrone-72) including the United Kingdom.[[n 6]](#cite_note-ECApp-74)[[69]](#cite_note-DCHCrown-75)[[70]](#cite_note-Romney273-76)[[71]](#cite_note-Buchan1969-77)[[72]](#cite_note-78) Only Canadian federal [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Crown "Minister of the Crown") may advise the sovereign on any and all matters of the Canadian state,[[n 7]](#cite_note-Black-79)[[70]](#cite_note-Romney273-76)[[73]](#cite_note-Buck2-80)[[74]](#cite_note-81)[[75]](#cite_note-82)[[76]](#cite_note-83)[[77]](#cite_note-84) of which the sovereign, when not in Canada, is kept abreast by weekly communications with the federal viceroy.[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85) The monarchy thus ceased to be an exclusively British institution and, in Canada, became a Canadian,[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40)[[79]](#cite_note-MacLeod3-86)[[80]](#cite_note-87)[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88) or "domesticated",[[82]](#cite_note-89) establishment, though it is still often denoted as "British" in both legal and common language,[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40) for reasons historical, political, and of convenience.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Airbus_CC-150_Polaris_at_Yellowknife_Airport.jpg/250px-Airbus_CC-150_Polaris_at_Yellowknife_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airbus_CC-150_Polaris_at_Yellowknife_Airport.jpg)

A [Royal Canadian Air Force Royal Flight](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force_VIP_aircraft "Royal Canadian Air Force VIP aircraft"), used to transport [the Duke](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales") and [Duchess of Cambridge](/wiki/Catherine,_Princess_of_Wales "Catherine, Princess of Wales") during their [2011 royal tour of Canada](/wiki/2011_royal_tour_of_Canada "2011 royal tour of Canada").

This division is illustrated in a number of ways: The sovereign, for example, holds [a unique Canadian title](/wiki/Title_and_style_of_the_Canadian_monarch "Title and style of the Canadian monarch") and,[[83]](#cite_note-90) when he and other members of the royal family are acting in public specifically as representatives of Canada, they use, where possible, Canadian symbols, including the country's [national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada"), [unique royal symbols](#Symbols,_associations,_and_awards), [armed forces uniforms](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Canadian_Forces "Uniforms of the Canadian Forces"),[[84]](#cite_note-91)[[85]](#cite_note-92)[[86]](#cite_note-93)[[87]](#cite_note-94) and the like, as well as Canadian Forces aircraft or other Canadian-owned vehicles for travel.[[88]](#cite_note-GMtravel-95) Once in Canadian airspace, or arrived at a Canadian event taking place abroad, the [Canadian secretary to the King](/wiki/Canadian_secretary_to_the_King "Canadian secretary to the King"), officers of the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police") (RCMP), and other Canadian officials will take over from whichever of their other realms' counterparts were previously escorting the King or other member of the royal family.[[88]](#cite_note-GMtravel-95)[[89]](#cite_note-Macleans-96)

The sovereign similarly only draws from Canadian funds for support in the performance of his duties when in Canada or acting as King of Canada abroad; Canadians do not pay any money to the King or any other member of the royal family, either towards personal income or to support royal residences outside of Canada.[[90]](#cite_note-2010TourFAQ-97)[[91]](#cite_note-CCC-98)

There are five aspects to the monarchy of Canada: constitutional (such as the use of the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative") in summoning and dissolving parliament, granting [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent")), national (delivering the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne") and the [Royal Christmas Message](/wiki/Royal_Christmas_Message "Royal Christmas Message"), distributing honours, decorations, and medals, and partaking in [Remembrance Day](/wiki/Remembrance_Day#Canada "Remembrance Day") ceremonies), international (the monarch being head of state in other Commonwealth realms, and being the [head of the Commonwealth](/wiki/Head_of_the_Commonwealth "Head of the Commonwealth")), religious (the words *[by the grace of God](/wiki/By_the_grace_of_God "By the grace of God")* in the [monarch's title](/wiki/Title_and_style_of_the_Canadian_monarch "Title and style of the Canadian monarch"), the *[Act of Settlement, 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement,_1701 "Act of Settlement, 1701")*, requiring the sovereign to be Anglican, and the monarch encouraging people "to tolerate, accept, and understand cultures, beliefs, and faiths different from our own"), and the welfare and service monarchy (seen in members of the [royal family](#Royal_family_and_house) founding charities and supporting others, fundraising for charity, and [giving royal patronage to civil and military organizations](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_organizations_with_royal_patronage "List of Canadian organizations with royal patronage")).[[92]](#cite_note-99)

### Succession and regency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Succession and regency")]

Further information: [Succession to the British throne](/wiki/Succession_to_the_British_throne "Succession to the British throne") and [Succession to the Throne Act, 2013](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Throne_Act,_2013 "Succession to the Throne Act, 2013")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Gerrit_van_Honthorst_%28c.1590-1592-1656%29_-_Electress_Sophia_%281630%E2%80%931714%29%2C_Princess_Palatine%2C_Consort_of_Ernest_Augustus%2C_Elector_of_Hanover_-_493063_-_National_Trust.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gerrit_van_Honthorst_(c.1590-1592-1656)_-_Electress_Sophia_(1630%E2%80%931714),_Princess_Palatine,_Consort_of_Ernest_Augustus,_Elector_of_Hanover_-_493063_-_National_Trust.jpg)

[Sophia, Electress of Hanover](/wiki/Sophia_of_Hanover "Sophia of Hanover"), from whom heirs to the throne must directly descend

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Prince_of_Wales_in_Normandy_2024.jpg/120px-Prince_of_Wales_in_Normandy_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_of_Wales_in_Normandy_2024.jpg)

[William, Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"), heir apparent to the Canadian throne

As in the other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms"), the current [heir apparent](/wiki/Heir_apparent "Heir apparent") to [the Canadian throne](/wiki/Thrones_of_Canada "Thrones of Canada") is [William, Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"), who is followed in the line of succession by his eldest child, [Prince George](/wiki/Prince_George_of_Wales "Prince George of Wales").

#### Demise of the Crown and accession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Demise of the Crown and accession")]

Main article: [Demise of the Crown](/wiki/Demise_of_the_Crown "Demise of the Crown")

Upon the death of the monarch, there is an immediate and automatic succession by the late sovereign's heir;[[67]](#cite_note-OCCanThrone-72)[[93]](#cite_note-NPdeath-100)[[94]](#cite_note-101) hence the phrase, "[the King is dead. Long live the King](/wiki/The_king_is_dead,_long_live_the_king! "The king is dead, long live the king!")".[[95]](#cite_note-102)[[96]](#cite_note-103) No confirmation or further ceremony is necessary. The federal [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada") and [civil service](/wiki/Public_Service_of_Canada "Public Service of Canada") follow the *Manual of Official Procedure of the Government of Canada* in carrying out various formalities around the transition.[[97]](#cite_note-Torrance37-104)

By custom, the accession of a new monarch is publicly [proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation "Proclamation") by the governor general-[in-council](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada"), who meet at [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall") immediately upon the previous monarch's death.[[97]](#cite_note-Torrance37-104) Since the adoption of the Statute of Westminster it has been considered "constitutionally inappropriate" for Canada's accession proclamations to be approved by a British order-in-council,[[66]](#cite_note-Torrance34-71) as the monarch has, since then, assumed the Canadian throne according to Canadian law. For the accession of Charles III, the first since the creation of the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority") in 1989, the [Chief Herald](/wiki/Chief_Herald_of_Canada "Chief Herald of Canada") read the royal proclamation aloud. If Parliament is in session, the Prime Minister will announce the demise of the Crown there and [move](/wiki/Motion_(parliamentary_procedure) "Motion (parliamentary procedure)") for a [joint address](/wiki/Joint_address_(Canada) "Joint address (Canada)") of sympathy and loyalty to the new monarch.[[97]](#cite_note-Torrance37-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Queen_Elizabeth_Ottawa_parade_mosbo6.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_Ottawa_parade_mosbo6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_Ottawa_parade_mosbo6.jpg)

A memorial procession in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") before the national commemoration ceremony for the death of Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")

A period of [mourning](/wiki/Mourning "Mourning") also follows, during which portraits of the recently deceased monarch are draped with black fabric and staff at government houses wear [black armbands](/wiki/Armband#Mourning "Armband"). The *Manual of Official Procedure of the Government of Canada* states the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") is responsible for convening Parliament, tabling a resolution of loyalty and condolence from Parliament to the new monarch, and arranging for the motion to be seconded by the [leader of the official opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Leader of the Official Opposition (Canada)").[[93]](#cite_note-NPdeath-100)[[98]](#cite_note-Manu-105) The prime minister will then move to adjourn Parliament.[[93]](#cite_note-NPdeath-100)[[98]](#cite_note-Manu-105) The [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") keeps a regularly updated plan for a "broadcast of national importance", announcing the demise of a sovereign and covering the aftermath, during which all regular programming and advertising is cancelled and on-call commentators contribute to a 24-hour news mode.[[93]](#cite_note-NPdeath-100) As funerals for Canada's sovereigns, as well as for their consorts, take place in the United Kingdom,[[99]](#cite_note-monarchs-106) [commemoration services](/wiki/State_funerals_in_Canada#Ceremonies_for_monarchs "State funerals in Canada") are conducted by the federal and provincial governments across Canada.[[99]](#cite_note-monarchs-106)[[100]](#cite_note-107) Such ceremonies may also be held for other recently deceased members of the royal family. The day of the sovereign's funeral is likely to be a federal holiday.[[93]](#cite_note-NPdeath-100)[[101]](#cite_note-108)

The new monarch is [crowned](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch") in the United Kingdom in an ancient ritual but one not necessary for a sovereign to reign.[[n 8]](#cite_note-EdVIII1-109) Under the federal *Interpretation Act*,[[97]](#cite_note-Torrance37-104) officials who hold a federal office under the Crown are not affected by the death of the monarch, nor are they required to take the [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Canada) "Oath of Allegiance (Canada)") again.[[102]](#cite_note-110) In some provinces, though, those holding Crown offices must swear the Oath to the new sovereign.[[103]](#cite_note-111) All references in federal legislation to previous monarchs, whether in the masculine (e.g. *His Majesty*) or feminine (e.g. *The Queen*), continue to mean the reigning sovereign of Canada, regardless of their gender.[[104]](#cite_note-InterpAct-112) This is because, in common law, [the Crown never dies](/wiki/Corporation_sole#The_Crown "Corporation sole"). After an individual accedes to the throne, he or she usually continues to reign until death.[[n 9]](#cite_note-EdVIII2-113)

#### Legal aspects of succession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legal aspects of succession")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Act_of_Settlement_3323.jpg/250px-Act_of_Settlement_3323.jpg)](/wiki/File:Act_of_Settlement_3323.jpg)

A copy of the *Act of Settlement, 1701*

The relationship between the Commonwealth realms is such that any change to the rules of succession to their respective crowns requires the unanimous consent of all the realms. Succession is governed by statutes, such as the *[Bill of Rights, 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689")*, the *[Act of Settlement, 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement,_1701 "Act of Settlement, 1701")*, and the *[Acts of Union, 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union,_1707 "Acts of Union, 1707")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/KingPrince1924.jpg/250px-KingPrince1924.jpg)](/wiki/File:KingPrince1924.jpg)

Prime Minister of Canada [Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King") (left) and [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") (right; when Prince of Wales) in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), 1924. In 1936, [Edward abdicated the Canadian throne](/wiki/Abdication_of_Edward_VIII "Abdication of Edward VIII") and he and any of his descendants were removed from the line of succession by Order-in-Council PC 3144 and the *[Succession to the Throne Act, 1937](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Throne_Act,_1937 "Succession to the Throne Act, 1937")*.

[Edward VIII abdicated in 1936](/wiki/Abdication_of_Edward_VIII "Abdication of Edward VIII"), and any possible future descendants of his were excluded from the line of succession.[[105]](#cite_note-114) The [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") at the time, wishing for speed so as to avoid embarrassing debate in Dominion parliaments, suggested that the governments of the Dominions of the British Commonwealth—then Australia, New Zealand, the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa"), and Canada—regard whoever was monarch of the UK to automatically be monarch of their respective Dominion. As with the other Dominion governments, the Canadian Cabinet, headed by Prime Minister [William Lyon Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King"), refused to accept the idea and stressed that the laws of succession were part of Canadian law and, as the Statute of Westminster 1931 disallowed the UK from legislating for Canada, including in relation to succession,[[106]](#cite_note-115) altering them required Canada's request and consent to the British legislation (*[His Majesty's Declaration of Abdication Act, 1936](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Declaration_of_Abdication_Act_1936 "His Majesty's Declaration of Abdication Act 1936")*) becoming part of Canadian law.[[107]](#cite_note-Twomeyvid-116) Sir [Maurice Gwyer](/wiki/Maurice_Gwyer "Maurice Gwyer"), [first parliamentary counsel](/wiki/Office_of_the_Parliamentary_Counsel_(United_Kingdom) "Office of the Parliamentary Counsel (United Kingdom)") in the UK, reflected this position, stating the *Act of Settlement* was a part of the law in each Dominion.[[107]](#cite_note-Twomeyvid-116) Thus, Order-in-Council P.C. 3144[[108]](#cite_note-117) was issued, expressing the Cabinet's request and consent for *His Majesty's Declaration of Abdication Act, 1936*, to become part of the laws of Canada and the *[Succession to the Throne Act, 1937](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Throne_Act,_1937 "Succession to the Throne Act, 1937")*, gave parliamentary ratification to that action, together bringing the *Act of Settlement* and *[Royal Marriages Act, 1772](/wiki/Royal_Marriages_Act_1772 "Royal Marriages Act 1772")*, into Canadian law.[[109]](#cite_note-118)[[110]](#cite_note-119) The latter was deemed by the Cabinet in 1947 to be part of Canadian law.[[n 10]](#cite_note-Marriages-122)[[111]](#cite_note-Boyce81-120) The [Department of External Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Foreign_Affairs,_Trade_and_Development "Department of Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development") included all succession-related laws in its list of acts within Canadian law.

The [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") declared unanimously in the 1981 *[Patriation Reference](/wiki/Patriation_Reference "Patriation Reference")* that the *Bill of Rights, 1689*, is "undoubtedly in force as part of the law of Canada".[[113]](#cite_note-123)[[114]](#cite_note-124) Furthermore, in *[O'Donohue v. Canada](/wiki/O%27Donohue_v._Canada "O'Donohue v. Canada")* (2003) the [Ontario Superior Court of Justice](/wiki/Ontario_Superior_Court_of_Justice "Ontario Superior Court of Justice") found that the *Act of Settlement, 1701*, is "part of the laws of Canada" and the rules of succession are "by necessity incorporated into the Constitution of Canada".[[115]](#cite_note-125) Another ruling of the Ontario Superior Court, in 2014, echoed the 2003 case, stating that the *Act of Settlement* "is an imperial statute which ultimately became part of the law of Canada."[[116]](#cite_note-Court-126) Upon dismissing appeal of that case, the Court of Appeal of Ontario stated "[t]he rules of succession are a part of the fabric of the constitution of Canada and incorporated into it".[[117]](#cite_note-127)

In a meeting of the Special Joint Committee on the Constitution during the process of [patriating](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation") the Canadian constitution in 1981, [John Munro](/wiki/John_Munro_(Canadian_politician) "John Munro (Canadian politician)") asked Minister of Justice [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien") about the "selective omissions" of the *Succession to the Throne Act, 1937*, the *[Demise of the Crown Act, 1901](/wiki/Demise_of_the_Crown_Act_1901 "Demise of the Crown Act 1901")*, the *Seals Act*, the *Governor General's Act*, and the *Royal Style and Titles Act, 1953*, from the schedule to the *[Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982")*. In response, Chrétien asserted that the schedule to the *Constitution Act, 1982*, was not exhaustive, outlining that section 52(2) of the *Constitution Act, 1982*, says "[t]he Constitution of Canada includes [...] the acts and orders referred to the schedule" and "[w]hen you use the word 'includes' [...] it means that if ever there is another thing related to the Canadian constitution as part of it, should have been there, or might have been there, it is covered. So we do not have to renumerate [sic] the ones that you are mentioning."[[118]](#cite_note-committee-128) In the same meeting, Deputy Attorney General [Barry Strayer](/wiki/Barry_Strayer "Barry Strayer") stated: "Clause 52(2) is not an exhaustive definition of the Constitution of Canada so that while we have certain things listed in the schedule which are clearly part of the constitution, that does not mean that there are not other things which are part of the constitution [...] [The schedule] is not an exhaustive list."[[118]](#cite_note-committee-128)

|  |

| {{{annotations}}}  The [throne of Canada](/wiki/Thrones_of_Canada#1878_thrones "Thrones of Canada") (left) and throne for the royal consort (right)—both commissioned in 1878—behind the speaker's chair in the [Senate](/wiki/Centre_Block#Senate_chamber "Centre Block") |

|  |
| --- |
| {{{annotations}}}  The [sovereign's throne](/wiki/Thrones_of_Canada#2017_thrones "Thrones of Canada") (left) and royal consort's throne (right) behind the speaker's chair—all made in 2017—in the [temporary Senate chamber](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada_Building#Temporary_Senate_of_Canada_building "Senate of Canada Building") |

Leslie Zines claimed in the 1991 publication, *Constitutional Change in the Commonwealth*, that, though the succession to Canada's throne was outlined by common law and the *Act of Settlement, 1701*, these were not part of the Canadian constitution, which "does not contain rules for succession to the throne."[[119]](#cite_note-129) Richard Toporoski, writing three years later for the [Monarchist League of Canada](/wiki/Monarchist_League_of_Canada "Monarchist League of Canada"), stated, "there is no existing provision in our law, other than the *Act of Settlement, 1701*, that provides that the king or queen of Canada shall be the same person as the king or queen of the United Kingdom. If the British law were to be changed and we did not change our law [...] the person provided for in the new law would become king or queen in at least some realms of the Commonwealth; Canada would continue on with the person who would have become monarch under the previous law."[[120]](#cite_note-Toporoski1998-130)

Canada, with the other Commonwealth realms, committed to the 2011 [Perth Agreement](/wiki/Perth_Agreement "Perth Agreement"), which proposed changes to the rules governing succession to remove male preference and removal of disqualification arising from marriage to a Roman Catholic. As a result, the Canadian Parliament passed the *[Succession to the Throne Act, 2013](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Throne_Act,_2013 "Succession to the Throne Act, 2013")*, which gave the country's assent to the *[Succession to the Crown Bill](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Crown_Act_2013 "Succession to the Crown Act 2013")*, at that time proceeding in the Parliament of the United Kingdom. In dismissing a challenge to the law on the basis that a change to the succession in Canada would require unanimous consent of all provinces under section 41(a) of the *Constitution Act, 1982*, Quebec Superior Court Justice Claude Bouchard ruled that Canada "did not have to change its laws nor its constitution for the British royal succession rules to be amended and effective" and [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)") committed Canada to having a line of succession symmetrical to those of other Commonwealth realms.[[121]](#cite_note-131)[[122]](#cite_note-quebecappeal-132) The ruling was upheld by the [Quebec Court of Appeal](/wiki/Quebec_Court_of_Appeal "Quebec Court of Appeal").[[123]](#cite_note-133) The Supreme Court of Canada declined to hear an appeal in April 2020.[[124]](#cite_note-scc-134)

Constitutional scholar Philippe Lagassé argues that, in light of the *Succession to the Throne Act, 2013*, and court rulings upholding that law, section 41(a) of the *Constitution Act, 1982*, which requires a constitutional amendment passed with the unanimous consent of the provinces, applies only to the "office of the Queen", but not who holds that office, and that therefore "ending the principle of symmetry with the United Kingdom can be done with the general amending procedure, or even by Parliament alone under section 44 of the *Constitution Act, 1982*."[[124]](#cite_note-scc-134)[[125]](#cite_note-lagasse-135)

[Ted McWhinney](/wiki/Ted_McWhinney "Ted McWhinney"), another constitutional scholar, argued that a future government of Canada could begin a process of phasing out the monarchy after the [death of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Death_of_Elizabeth_II "Death of Elizabeth II") "quietly and without fanfare by simply failing legally to proclaim any successor to the Queen in relation to Canada". This would, he claimed, be a way of bypassing the need for a constitutional amendment that would require unanimous consent by the federal Parliament and all the provincial legislatures.[[126]](#cite_note-136) However, Ian Holloway, Dean of Law at the [University of Western Ontario](/wiki/University_of_Western_Ontario "University of Western Ontario"), criticized McWhinney's proposal for its ignorance of provincial input and opined that its implementation "would be contrary to the plain purpose of those who framed our system of government."[[127]](#cite_note-137)

Certain aspects of the succession rules have been challenged in the courts. For example, under the provisions of the *Bill of Rights, 1689*, and the *Act of Settlement, 1701*, Catholics are barred from succeeding to the throne; this prohibition has been upheld twice by Canadian courts, once in [2003](/wiki/O%27Donohue_v_Canada "O'Donohue v Canada") and again in 2014.[[128]](#cite_note-ctvteskey-138)[[129]](#cite_note-139)[[130]](#cite_note-140)[[131]](#cite_note-141) Legal scholar Christopher Cornell of the [SMU Dedman School of Law](/wiki/Dedman_School_of_Law "Dedman School of Law") concluded "that the prohibition on the Canadian Monarch being Catholic, while discriminatory, is perfectly-if not fundamentally-constitutional" and that if the prohibition is "to be changed or removed it will have to be accomplished politically and legislatively through another multilateral agreement similar to the Perth Agreement rather than judicially through the courts."[[132]](#cite_note-142)

#### Regency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Regency")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_-_George_IV%2C_1762_-_1830._Reigned_as_Regent_1811_-_1820%2C_as_King_1820_-_1830_-_PG_139_-_National_Galleries_of_Scotland.jpg/250px-Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_-_George_IV%2C_1762_-_1830._Reigned_as_Regent_1811_-_1820%2C_as_King_1820_-_1830_-_PG_139_-_National_Galleries_of_Scotland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_-_George_IV,_1762_-_1830._Reigned_as_Regent_1811_-_1820,_as_King_1820_-_1830_-_PG_139_-_National_Galleries_of_Scotland.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Thomas_Gainsborough_%281727-88%29_-_George_III_%281738-1820%29_-_RCIN_401006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/120px-Thomas_Gainsborough_%281727-88%29_-_George_III_%281738-1820%29_-_RCIN_401006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough_(1727-88)_-_George_III_(1738-1820)_-_RCIN_401006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

[Prince George, Prince Regent](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") (left), Canada's last regent, serving as such from 1811 until the death of his father, [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") (right), in 1820

Canada has no laws allowing for a [regency](/wiki/Regency "Regency"), should the sovereign be a [minor](/wiki/Minor_(law) "Minor (law)") or debilitated;[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85) none have been passed by the Canadian Parliament and it was made clear by successive cabinets since 1937 that the United Kingdom's [Regency Act](/wiki/Regency_Acts "Regency Acts") had no applicability to Canada,[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85) as the Canadian Cabinet had not requested otherwise when the act was passed that year and again in 1943 and 1953. As the *[Letters Patent, 1947](/wiki/Letters_Patent,_1947 "Letters Patent, 1947")*, issued by King George VI permit the governor general of Canada to exercise almost all of the monarch's powers in respect of Canada, the viceroy is expected to continue to act as the personal representative of the monarch, and not any regent, even if the monarch is a child or incapacitated.[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85)[[133]](#cite_note-143)[[134]](#cite_note-CRHT2-144)

This has led to the question of whether the governor general has the ability to remove themselves and appoint their viceregal successor in the monarch's name. While Lagassé argued that appears to be the case,[[125]](#cite_note-lagasse-135) both the *Canadian Manual of Official Procedures*, published in 1968, and the Privy Council Office took the opposite opinion.[[135]](#cite_note-145)[[136]](#cite_note-146) Lagassé and Patrick Baud claimed changes could be made to regulations to allow a governor general to appoint the next governor general;[[137]](#cite_note-147) [Christopher McCreery](/wiki/Christopher_McCreery "Christopher McCreery"), however, criticized the theory, arguing it is impractical to suggest that a governor general would remove him or herself on ministerial advice,[[138]](#cite_note-148) with the consequence that, if a prolonged regency occurred, it would remove one of the checks and balances in the constitution.[[139]](#cite_note-Twomey52-149) The intent expressed whenever the matter of regency came up among Commonwealth realm heads of government was that the relevant parliament (other than the United Kingdom's) would pass a bill if the need for a regency arose and the pertinent governor-general would already be empowered to grant royal assent to it.[[140]](#cite_note-150) The governor general appointing their successor is not a power that has been utilized to date.[[125]](#cite_note-lagasse-135)

## Federal and provincial aspects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Federal and provincial aspects")]

Further information: [Monarchy in the Canadian provinces](/wiki/Monarchy_in_the_Canadian_provinces "Monarchy in the Canadian provinces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Canada%27s_Governor_General%2C_Lieutenant_Governors%2C_Territorial_Commissioners_and_their_Private_Secretaries%2C_2016.jpg/250px-Canada%27s_Governor_General%2C_Lieutenant_Governors%2C_Territorial_Commissioners_and_their_Private_Secretaries%2C_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canada%27s_Governor_General,_Lieutenant_Governors,_Territorial_Commissioners_and_their_Private_Secretaries,_2016.jpg)

The one federal and 10 provincial viceroys, along with the three territorial commissioners, at their annual meeting in 2016, accompanied by their respective partners and secretaries

The origins of [Canadian sovereignty](/wiki/Canadian_sovereignty "Canadian sovereignty") lie in the early 17th century, during which time the monarch in England fought with parliament there over who had ultimate authority, culminating in the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") in 1688 and the subsequent *Bill of Rights, 1689*, which, as mentioned elsewhere in this article, is today part of Canadian constitutional law. This brought to Canada the British notion of the [supremacy of parliament](/wiki/Parliamentary_supremacy "Parliamentary supremacy")—of which the monarch is a part—and it was carried into each of the provinces upon the implementation of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"). That, however, was superseded when the *[Charter of Rights and Freedoms](/wiki/Canadian_Charter_of_Rights_and_Freedoms "Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms")* (within the *Constitution Act, 1982*) introduced into Canada the American idea of the [supremacy of the law](/wiki/Supremacy_Clause "Supremacy Clause").[[159]](#cite_note-169) Still, the King remains the [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") of Canada.[[n 11]](#cite_note-Sovereignty-171)[[161]](#cite_note-172)

Canada's monarchy was established at Confederation, when its executive government and authority were declared, in section 9 of the *Constitution Act, 1867*, to continue and be vested in the monarch. Placing such power, along with legislative power, with the tangible, living Queen, rather than the abstract and inanimate Crown, was a deliberate choice by the framers of the constitution.[[162]](#cite_note-173) Still, the Crown is the foundation of the country[[163]](#cite_note-Tidridge16-174)[[164]](#cite_note-Johnstonxi-175) as "the very centre of [Canada's] constitution and democracy."[[163]](#cite_note-Tidridge16-174) Although Canada is a federation, the Canadian monarchy is unitary throughout all jurisdictions in the country,[[165]](#cite_note-Roberts13-176) the sovereignty of the different administrations being passed on through the overreaching Crown itself as a part of the executive, legislative, and judicial operations in each of the federal and provincial spheres[[166]](#cite_note-177) and the [headship of state](/wiki/Headship_of_state "Headship of state") being a part of all equally.[[165]](#cite_note-Roberts13-176) The Crown thus links the various governments into a federal state,[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7) while it is simultaneously also "divided" into 11 legal jurisdictions, or 11 "crowns"—one federal and 10 provincial[[167]](#cite_note-178)—with the monarch taking on a distinct [legal persona](/wiki/Legal_person "Legal person") in each.[[n 12]](#cite_note-Pers2-180)[[n 13]](#cite_note-OMNR-182) As such, the constitution instructs that any change to the position of the monarch or their representatives in Canada requires the consent of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada"), and the legislative assemblies of all the provinces.[[170]](#cite_note-ElizabethII1982-183) The Crown, being shared and balanced,[[164]](#cite_note-Johnstonxi-175) provides the bedrock upon which all of Canada's different regions and peoples can live together peacefully[[171]](#cite_note-184) and was said by David E. Smith, in 2017, to be the "keystone of the constitutional architecture" of Canada.[[172]](#cite_note-185)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/DSC01923_-_Royal_Window_%2829867749537%29.jpg/250px-DSC01923_-_Royal_Window_%2829867749537%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSC01923_-_Royal_Window_(29867749537).jpg)

The Royal Window in the [foyer](/wiki/Rideau_Hall#The_McKay_villa_and_Mappin_block "Rideau Hall") of [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall"), commemorating Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II")'s [Ruby Jubilee](/wiki/Ruby_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Ruby Jubilee of Elizabeth II"). At centre is the [Royal Coat of Arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada") surrounded by the shields of each of the sovereign's provincial and territorial coats of arms.

The Crown is located beyond politics, existing to give authority to and protect the constitution and system of governance.[[163]](#cite_note-Tidridge16-174) Power, therefore, rests with an institution that "functions to safeguard it on behalf of all its citizens", rather than any singular individual.[[173]](#cite_note-186) The sovereign and his representatives typically "act by 'not acting'"[[n 14]](#cite_note-REH-188)—holding power, but, not exercising it—both because they are unelected figures and to maintain their neutrality, "deliberately, insistently, and resolutely",[[175]](#cite_note-189) in case they have to be an impartial arbiter in a constitutional crisis and ensure that normal democratic discourse can resume.[[176]](#cite_note-190)[[174]](#cite_note-Hawkins104-187)[[177]](#cite_note-191) Consequently, the Crown performs two functions:[[178]](#cite_note-Jackson11-13-192) as a unifying symbol and a protector of democratic rights and freedoms,[[164]](#cite_note-Johnstonxi-175) "tightly woven into the fabric of the Canadian constitution."[[178]](#cite_note-Jackson11-13-192)

At the same time, a number of freedoms granted by the constitution to all other Canadians are denied to, or limited for, the monarch and the other senior members of the royal family: freedom of religion, freedom of expression, freedom to travel, freedom to choose a career, freedom to marry, and freedom of privacy and family life.[[179]](#cite_note-193)

While the Crown is empowered by statute and the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"), it also enjoys inherent powers not granted by either.[[180]](#cite_note-194) The [Court of Appeal of British Columbia](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_British_Columbia "Court of Appeal of British Columbia") ruled in 1997 that "the Crown has the capacities and powers of a natural person"[[181]](#cite_note-195) and its actions as a natural person are, as with the actions of any natural person, subject to judicial review.[[182]](#cite_note-196) Further, it was determined in *R. v Secretary of State for Health the ex parte C* that, "as a matter of capacity, no doubt, [the Crown] has power to do whatever a private person can do. But, as an organ of government, it can only exercise those powers for the public benefit, and for identifiably 'governmental' purposes within limits set by the law."[[183]](#cite_note-197) Similarly, use of the royal prerogative is justiciable,[[184]](#cite_note-198) though, only when the "subject matter affects the rights or legitimate expectations of an individual".[[185]](#cite_note-199)

The governor general is appointed by the monarch on the advice of his federal prime minister and the lieutenant governors are appointed by the governor general on the advice of the federal prime minister. The [commissioners](/wiki/Commissioner "Commissioner") of Canada's territories are appointed by the federal governor-in-council, at the recommendation of the [minister of Crown–Indigenous relations](/wiki/Minister_of_Crown%E2%80%93Indigenous_relations "Minister of Crown–Indigenous relations"), but, as the territories are not sovereign entities, the commissioners are not personal representatives of the sovereign. The [Advisory Committee on Vice-Regal Appointments](/wiki/Advisory_Committee_on_Vice-Regal_Appointments "Advisory Committee on Vice-Regal Appointments"), which may seek input from the relevant premier and provincial or territorial community, proposes candidates for appointment as governor general, lieutenant governor, and commissioner.[[186]](#cite_note-200)[[187]](#cite_note-201)

### Sovereign immunity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sovereign immunity")]

It has been held since 1918 that the federal Crown is immune from provincial law.[[188]](#cite_note-202) Constitutional convention has also held that the Crown in right of each province is outside the jurisdiction of the courts in other provinces. This view, however, has been questioned.[[189]](#cite_note-203)

Lieutenant governors do not enjoy the same immunity as the sovereign in matters not relating to the powers of the viceregal office, as decided in the case of former Lieutenant Governor of Quebec [Lise Thibault](/wiki/Lise_Thibault "Lise Thibault"), who had been accused of misappropriating public funds.[[190]](#cite_note-204)

## Personification of the Canadian state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personification of the Canadian state")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Stamp_1968_CAN_MiNr0429Ax_pm_B002a.jpg/250px-Stamp_1968_CAN_MiNr0429Ax_pm_B002a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_1968_CAN_MiNr0429Ax_pm_B002a.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0484x_pm_B002.jpg/250px-Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0484x_pm_B002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0484x_pm_B002.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Stamp_1967_CAN_MiNr402Ax_pm_B002.jpg/250px-Stamp_1967_CAN_MiNr402Ax_pm_B002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_1967_CAN_MiNr402Ax_pm_B002.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0494AxDF_pm_B002.jpg/250px-Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0494AxDF_pm_B002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_1971_CAN_MiNr0494AxDF_pm_B002.jpg)

[Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), Queen of Canada, depicted on various [Canadian stamps](/wiki/Postage_stamps_and_postal_history_of_Canada "Postage stamps and postal history of Canada") through the 1960s and 1970s

As the living embodiment of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"),[[104]](#cite_note-InterpAct-112)[[191]](#cite_note-205) the sovereign is regarded as the [personification](/wiki/Personification "Personification") of the Canadian [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)")[[n 15]](#cite_note-Pers1-210)[[29]](#cite_note-SKEX10-29)[[73]](#cite_note-Buck2-80)[[192]](#cite_note-DCH3-206)[[196]](#cite_note-Bell125-211)[[197]](#cite_note-212)[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[199]](#cite_note-214)[[200]](#cite_note-215)[[201]](#cite_note-216)[[202]](#cite_note-217)[[203]](#cite_note-218)[[204]](#cite_note-219) and is meant to represent all Canadians, regardless of political affiliation.[[205]](#cite_note-220) As such, he, along with their viceregal representatives, must "remain strictly neutral in political terms".[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88)

The person of the reigning sovereign thus holds two distinct personas in constant coexistence, an ancient theory of the "King's two bodies"—the body natural (subject to infirmity and death) and the body politic (which never dies).[[206]](#cite_note-221) The Crown and the monarch are "conceptually divisible but legally indivisible [...] The office cannot exist without the office-holder",[[n 16]](#cite_note-Boyce-223)[[67]](#cite_note-OCCanThrone-72) so, even in private, the monarch is always "on duty".[[196]](#cite_note-Bell125-211) The terms *the state*, *the Crown*,[[208]](#cite_note-224) *the Crown in Right of Canada*, *His Majesty the King in Right of Canada* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Sa Majesté le Roi du chef du Canada*),[[209]](#cite_note-225) and similar are all synonymous and the monarch's [legal personality](/wiki/Legal_personality "Legal personality") is sometimes referred to simply as *Canada*.[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[210]](#cite_note-226)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/1TS_0197_%2816440153338%29.jpg/250px-1TS_0197_%2816440153338%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1TS_0197_(16440153338).jpg)

A photo portrait of Queen Elizabeth II stands at the front of a citizenship ceremony led by [Lieutenant Governor of Ontario](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Ontario "Lieutenant Governor of Ontario") [Elizabeth Dowdeswell](/wiki/Elizabeth_Dowdeswell "Elizabeth Dowdeswell"), illustrating the sovereign as the focus of the [Oath of Citizenship](/wiki/Oath_of_Citizenship_(Canada) "Oath of Citizenship (Canada)")

The monarch is at the apex of the [Canadian order of precedence](/wiki/Canadian_order_of_precedence "Canadian order of precedence") and, as the embodiment of the state, is also the focus of oaths of allegiance,[[n 17]](#cite_note-Oath-228)[[192]](#cite_note-DCH3-206)[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[212]](#cite_note-CIC29-229)[[213]](#cite_note-230) required of many of the aforementioned employees of the Crown, as well as by new [citizens](/wiki/Citizenship "Citizenship"), as by the [Oath of Citizenship](/wiki/Oath_of_citizenship_(Canada) "Oath of citizenship (Canada)"). Allegiance is given in [reciprocation](/wiki/Reciprocal_(grammar) "Reciprocal (grammar)") to the sovereign's [Coronation](/wiki/Coronation "Coronation") Oath,[[214]](#cite_note-231) wherein he or she promises to govern the people of Canada "according to their respective laws and customs".[[215]](#cite_note-232)

### Head of state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Head of state")]

Although it has been argued that the term *head of state* is a republican one inapplicable in a constitutional monarchy such as Canada, where the monarch is the embodiment of the state and thus cannot be head of it,[[196]](#cite_note-Bell125-211) the sovereign is regarded by official government sources,[[38]](#cite_note-DCHMon-38)[[216]](#cite_note-Buck1-233)[[217]](#cite_note-234)[[218]](#cite_note-235) judges,[[219]](#cite_note-236) constitutional scholars,[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[220]](#cite_note-237) and pollsters as the head of state,[[221]](#cite_note-IR2008-238) while the governor general and lieutenant governors are all only representatives of, and thus equally subordinate to, that figure.[[222]](#cite_note-Boswell-239) Some governors general, their staff, government publications,[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213) and constitutional scholars like Ted McWhinney and C.E.S. Franks have,[[223]](#cite_note-240)[[224]](#cite_note-Franks-241) however, referred to the position of governor general as that of Canada's head of state;[[225]](#cite_note-242)[[226]](#cite_note-243) though, sometimes qualifying the assertion with *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* or *effective*;[[227]](#cite_note-244)[[228]](#cite_note-245)[[229]](#cite_note-246) Franks has hence recommended that the governor general be named officially as the head of state.[[224]](#cite_note-Franks-241) Still others view the role of head of state as being shared by both the sovereign and his viceroys.[[230]](#cite_note-247)[[231]](#cite_note-248)[[232]](#cite_note-Boyce-249) Since 1927, governors general have been received on [state visits](/wiki/State_visit "State visit") abroad as though they were heads of state.[[233]](#cite_note-250)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/The_Governor_General_of_Canada%2C_Mr._David_Johnston_inspecting_the_Guard_of_Honour%2C_at_the_Ceremonial_Reception%2C_at_Rashtrapati_Bhavan%2C_in_New_Delhi_on_February_24%2C_2014.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Governor_General_of_Canada,_Mr._David_Johnston_inspecting_the_Guard_of_Honour,_at_the_Ceremonial_Reception,_at_Rashtrapati_Bhavan,_in_New_Delhi_on_February_24,_2014.jpg)

Governor General [David Johnston](/wiki/David_Johnston_(governor_general) "David Johnston (governor general)") reviews the guard of honour at [Rashtrapati Bhavan](/wiki/Rashtrapati_Bhavan,_New_Delhi "Rashtrapati Bhavan, New Delhi") during a state visit to India, 24 February 2014

Officials at Rideau Hall have attempted to use the *Letters Patent, 1947*, as justification for describing the governor general as head of state. However, the document makes no such distinction,[[234]](#cite_note-Coup-251) nor does it effect an abdication of the sovereign's powers in favour of the viceroy,[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85) as it only allows the governor general to "act on the Queen's behalf".[[235]](#cite_note-252)[[236]](#cite_note-253) D. Michael Jackson, former Chief of Protocol of Saskatchewan, argued that Rideau Hall had been attempting to "recast" the governor general as head of state since the 1970s and doing so preempted both the Queen and all of the lieutenant governors.[[222]](#cite_note-Boswell-239) This caused not only "precedence wars" at provincial events (where the governor general usurped the lieutenant governor's proper spot as most senior official in attendance)[[237]](#cite_note-254)[[238]](#cite_note-255) and Governor General Adrienne Clarkson to accord herself precedence before the Queen at a national occasion,[[239]](#cite_note-256) but also constitutional issues by "unbalancing [...] the federalist symmetry".[[165]](#cite_note-Roberts13-176)[[240]](#cite_note-GardnerRight-257) This has been regarded as both a natural evolution and as a dishonest effort to alter the constitution without public scrutiny.[[234]](#cite_note-Coup-251)[[241]](#cite_note-258)

In a poll conducted by [Ipsos-Reid](/wiki/Ipsos-Reid "Ipsos-Reid") following the [first prorogation of the 40th parliament](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932009_Canadian_parliamentary_dispute#Governor_General_prorogues_parliament "2008–2009 Canadian parliamentary dispute") on 4 December 2008, it was found that 42 per cent of the sample group thought the prime minister was head of state, while 33 per cent felt it was the governor general. Only 24 per cent named the Queen as head of state,[[221]](#cite_note-IR2008-238) a number up from 2002, when the results of an [EKOS Research Associates](/wiki/EKOS_Research_Associates "EKOS Research Associates") survey showed only 5 per cent of those polled knew the Queen was head of state (69 per cent answered that it was the prime minister).[[242]](#cite_note-259)

### Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Arms")]

Main articles: [Coat of arms of Canada](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Canada "Coat of arms of Canada") and [Royal flags of Canada](/wiki/Royal_flags_of_Canada "Royal flags of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Royal_Standard_of_Canada_UPDATED.svg/250px-Royal_Standard_of_Canada_UPDATED.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Standard_of_Canada_UPDATED.svg)

The [Sovereign's Flag for Canada](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Flag_for_Canada "Sovereign's Flag for Canada") employing the shield of the royal arms in banner form

The official [coat of arms of Canada](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Canada "Coat of arms of Canada") is the [arms of dominion](/wiki/Arms_of_dominion "Arms of dominion") of the Canadian monarch[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETidridge201171-260)[[244]](#cite_note-261) and a symbol of [national sovereignty](/wiki/Canadian_sovereignty "Canadian sovereignty").[[245]](#cite_note-262) It is closely modelled after the [royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom"), with French and distinctive Canadian elements replacing or added to those derived from the British version, which was employed in Canada before the granting of the Canadian arms in 1921.[[246]](#cite_note-263)

The [Sovereign's Flag for Canada](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Flag_for_Canada "Sovereign's Flag for Canada") is the monarch's official flag, which depicts the royal arms in [banner form](/wiki/Banner_of_arms "Banner of arms").[[247]](#cite_note-CHA-264) It takes precedence above all other flags in Canada—including the [national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") and those of the other members of the royal family[[38]](#cite_note-DCHMon-38)—and is typically flown from buildings, vessels, and vehicles in which the sovereign is present (although exceptions have been made for its use when the monarch is not in attendance). The sovereign's flag is never flown at half-mast because there is always a sovereign: when one dies, their successor becomes the sovereign instantly. Elements of the royal arms have also been incorporated into the [governor general's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_governor_general_of_Canada "Flag of the governor general of Canada"); similarly, the [flags of the lieutenant governors](/wiki/Flags_of_the_lieutenant_governors_of_Canada "Flags of the lieutenant governors of Canada") employ the shields of the relevant provincial coat of arms.

## Federal constitutional role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Federal constitutional role")]

[Canada's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") is based on the [Westminster parliamentary model](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), wherein the role of the King is both legal and practical, but not political.[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88) The sovereign is vested with all the powers of state, collectively known as the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"),[[248]](#cite_note-PCO-265) leading the populace to be considered subjects of the Crown.[[249]](#cite_note-266) However, as the sovereign's power stems from [the people](/wiki/People#Legal "People")[[26]](#cite_note-Forseyp1-26)[[250]](#cite_note-267) and the monarch is a [constitutional one](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), he or she does not rule alone, as in an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"). Instead, the Crown is regarded as a [corporation sole](/wiki/Corporation_sole "Corporation sole"), with the monarch being the centre of a construct in which the power of the whole is shared by multiple institutions of government[[163]](#cite_note-Tidridge16-174)—the [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)"), [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature"), and [judicial](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary")[[8]](#cite_note-DCH20094-8)—acting under the sovereign's authority,[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[251]](#cite_note-Murdoch-268) which is entrusted for exercise by the politicians (the elected and appointed parliamentarians and the ministers of the Crown generally drawn from among them) and the [judges](/wiki/Court_system_of_Canada "Court system of Canada") and [justices of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_Peace#Canada "Justice of the Peace").[[25]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-25) The monarchy has thus been described as the underlying principle of Canada's institutional unity and the monarch as a "guardian of constitutional freedoms"[[42]](#cite_note-Senate-42)[[212]](#cite_note-CIC29-229) whose "job is to ensure that the political process remains intact and is allowed to function."[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Canada_Great_Seal_Grand_Sceau.svg/250px-Canada_Great_Seal_Grand_Sceau.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Canada_Great_Seal_Grand_Sceau.svg)

A rendition of the Great Seal of Canada used during the reign of King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")

The [Great Seal of Canada](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Canada "Great Seal of Canada") "signifies the power and authority of the Crown flowing from the sovereign to [the] parliamentary government"[[252]](#cite_note-GSoC-269) and is applied to state documents such as royal proclamations and letters patent commissioning Cabinet ministers, senators, judges, and other senior government officials.[[253]](#cite_note-DCHGSoC-270) The "lending" of royal authority to Cabinet is illustrated by the great seal being entrusted by the governor general, the official keeper of the seal, to the [minister of innovation, science, and economic development](/wiki/Minister_of_Innovation,_Science,_and_Economic_Development "Minister of Innovation, Science, and Economic Development"), who is *ex officio* the [registrar general of Canada](/wiki/Registrar_General_of_Canada "Registrar General of Canada").[[253]](#cite_note-DCHGSoC-270) Upon a change of government, the seal is temporarily returned to the governor general and then "lent" to the next incoming registrar general.[[252]](#cite_note-GSoC-269)

The Crown is the pinnacle of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces"), with the constitution placing the monarch in the position of [commander-in-chief of the entire force](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief_of_the_Canadian_Forces "Commander-in-Chief of the Canadian Forces"), though the governor general carries out the duties attached to the position and also bears the title of *Commander-in-Chief in and over Canada*.[[254]](#cite_note-271)

### Executive (King-in-Council)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Executive (King-in-Council)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Queen_Elizabeth_and_members_of_the_federal_government_of_Canada_in_Ottawa_1957-10-14.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_and_members_of_the_federal_government_of_Canada_in_Ottawa_1957-10-14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_and_members_of_the_federal_government_of_Canada_in_Ottawa_1957-10-14.jpg)

The first meeting of the [Queen's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/Queen%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "Queen's Privy Council for Canada") before the reigning sovereign, Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), in the State Dining Room of Rideau Hall, 14 October 1957

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Her_Majesty_Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_her_Canadian_Ministers_at_Rideau_Hall_1_July_1967.jpg/250px-Her_Majesty_Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_her_Canadian_Ministers_at_Rideau_Hall_1_July_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Her_Majesty_Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_her_Canadian_Ministers_at_Rideau_Hall_1_July_1967.jpg)

Elizabeth II with her [Cabinet](/wiki/19th_Canadian_Ministry "19th Canadian Ministry") in the ballroom of Rideau Hall, on [Dominion Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day"), 1 July 1967, the [centennial of Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Centennial "Canadian Centennial")

The [government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada")—formally termed *[His Majesty's Government](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Government_(term) "His Majesty's Government (term)")*[[255]](#cite_note-MacLeod18-272)—is defined by the constitution as the King acting on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") of his Privy Council;[[255]](#cite_note-MacLeod18-272)[[256]](#cite_note-273)[[257]](#cite_note-MarleauExecutive-274) what is technically known as the *[King-in-Council](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada#King-in-Council "King's Privy Council for Canada")*,[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7) or sometimes the *Governor-in-Council*,[[104]](#cite_note-InterpAct-112) referring to the governor general as the King's stand-in, though, a few tasks must be specifically performed by, or bills that require assent from, the King.[[78]](#cite_note-Heard-85)[[258]](#cite_note-ECF-275)[[259]](#cite_note-276) One of the main duties of the Crown is to "ensure that a democratically elected government is always in place,"[[232]](#cite_note-Boyce-249) which means appointing a [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") to thereafter head the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada")[[260]](#cite_note-GG-277)—a committee of the Privy Council charged with advising the Crown on the exercise of the royal prerogative.[[257]](#cite_note-MarleauExecutive-274) The monarch is informed by his viceroy of the swearing-in and resignation of prime ministers and other members of the ministry,[[260]](#cite_note-GG-277) remains fully briefed through regular communications from his Canadian ministers, and holds audience with them whenever possible.[[216]](#cite_note-Buck1-233) By convention, the content of these communications and meetings remains confidential so as to protect the impartiality of the monarch and his representative.[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88)[[261]](#cite_note-278) The appropriateness and viability of this tradition in an age of [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") has been questioned.[[262]](#cite_note-279)[[263]](#cite_note-280)

In the construct of constitutional monarchy and [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"), the ministerial advice tendered is typically binding,[[264]](#cite_note-281) meaning the monarch *reigns* but does not *rule*,[[265]](#cite_note-Cyr-282) the Cabinet ruling "in trust" for the monarch.[[266]](#cite_note-283) This has been the case in Canada since the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") ended the reign of the territory's last [absolute monarch](/wiki/Absolute_monarch "Absolute monarch"), King [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV") of France. However, the royal prerogative belongs to the Crown and not to any of the ministers[[25]](#cite_note-MacLeod16-25)[[251]](#cite_note-Murdoch-268)[[267]](#cite_note-Neitsch23-284) and the royal and viceroyal figures may unilaterally use these powers in exceptional [constitutional crisis](/wiki/Constitutional_crisis "Constitutional crisis") situations (an exercise of the [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power")),[[n 18]](#cite_note-RP-285) thereby allowing the monarch to make sure "the government conducts itself in compliance with the constitution";[[232]](#cite_note-Boyce-249) he and the viceroys being guarantors of the government's constitutional, as opposed to democratic, legitimacy and must ensure the continuity of such.[[268]](#cite_note-286) Use of the royal prerogative in this manner was seen when [the Governor General refused his prime minister's advice to dissolve Parliament](/wiki/King%E2%80%93Byng_affair "King–Byng affair") in 1926 and when, in 2008, the Governor General took some hours to decide whether or not to accept her Prime Minister's advice to prorogue Parliament to avoid a vote of non-confidence.[[269]](#cite_note-ISCC-287)[[270]](#cite_note-288) The prerogative powers have also been [used numerous times in the provinces](/wiki/Monarchy_in_the_Canadian_provinces#Executive "Monarchy in the Canadian provinces").[[269]](#cite_note-ISCC-287)

The royal prerogative further extends to foreign affairs, including the ratification of treaties, alliances, international agreements, and [declarations of war](/wiki/Declaration_of_war_by_Canada "Declaration of war by Canada"),[[271]](#cite_note-289) the accreditation of Canadian high commissioners and ambassadors and receipt of similar diplomats from foreign states,[[272]](#cite_note-290)[[273]](#cite_note-CMN23-291) and the issuance of [Canadian passports](/wiki/Canadian_passport "Canadian passport"),[[274]](#cite_note-CPO-292) which remain the sovereign's property.[[275]](#cite_note-293) It also includes the creation of [dynastic](/wiki/Dynastic_order_of_knighthood "Dynastic order of knighthood") and [national](/wiki/Order_(honour) "Order (honour)") [honours](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Canada "Orders, decorations, and medals of Canada"),[[276]](#cite_note-294) though only the latter are established on official ministerial advice.

### Parliament (King-in-Parliament)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Parliament (King-in-Parliament)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/RoyalVisitSenate.jpg/250px-RoyalVisitSenate.jpg)](/wiki/File:RoyalVisitSenate.jpg)

King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), with [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother"), grants [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to bills in the [Senate chamber](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), 1939

All laws in Canada are the monarch's and the sovereign is one of the three components of the Parliament of Canada[[277]](#cite_note-295)[[278]](#cite_note-296)—formally called the *[King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament")*[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7)—but, the monarch and viceroy do not participate in the legislative process, save for [royal consent](/wiki/King%27s_Consent "King's Consent"), typically expressed by a minister of the Crown,[[279]](#cite_note-297) and [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"), which is necessary for a bill to be enacted as law. Either figure or a delegate may perform this task and the constitution allows the viceroy the option of deferring assent to the sovereign.[[280]](#cite_note-298)

The governor general is further responsible for summoning the House of Commons, while either the viceroy or monarch can [prorogue](/wiki/Prorogation_in_Canada "Prorogation in Canada") and [dissolve](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Parliament "Dissolution of Parliament") the legislature, after which the governor general usually [calls for a general election](/wiki/Writ_of_election "Writ of election"). This element of the royal prerogative is unaffected by legislation ["fixing" election dates](/wiki/Fixed_election_dates_in_Canada "Fixed election dates in Canada"), as *An Act to Amend the Canada Elections Act* specifies that it does not curtail the Crown's powers.[[281]](#cite_note-299) The new parliamentary session is marked by either the monarch, governor general, or some other representative reading the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne").[[282]](#cite_note-300) Members of Parliament must recite the Oath of Allegiance before they may take their seat. Further, the [official opposition](/wiki/Official_Opposition_(Canada) "Official Opposition (Canada)") is traditionally dubbed as *[His Majesty's Loyal Opposition](/wiki/Loyal_opposition "Loyal opposition")*,[[212]](#cite_note-CIC29-229)[[283]](#cite_note-301)[[284]](#cite_note-302) illustrating that, while its members are opposed to the incumbent government, they remain loyal to the sovereign (as personification of the state and its authority).[[285]](#cite_note-303)

The monarch does not have the prerogative to impose and collect new taxes without the authorization of an [act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament "Act of Parliament"). The consent of the Crown must, however, be obtained before either of the houses of Parliament may even debate a bill affecting the sovereign's prerogatives or interests and no act of Parliament binds the King or his rights unless the act states that it does.[[286]](#cite_note-304)

### Courts (King-on-the-Bench)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Courts (King-on-the-Bench)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/The_Nine.jpg/250px-The_Nine.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Nine.jpg)

A [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada") courtroom displaying on the focal wall a rendition of the Royal Arms.

The sovereign is traditionally viewed as the fount of justice, although practically speaking, this role is administered by justices in the monarch's name.[[287]](#cite_note-305)[[288]](#cite_note-306) The sovereign's position in the [Canadian courts](/wiki/Court_system_of_Canada "Court system of Canada") formally dubbed the *King on the Bench*.[[7]](#cite_note-MacLeod17-7) The [Arms of His Majesty in Right of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada"), provincial coats of arms[[289]](#cite_note-307) or the UK royal arms (for older court rooms) are displayed in Canadian courtrooms,[[290]](#cite_note-308) as is a portrait of the sovereign.[[291]](#cite_note-309)

Common law holds the notion that the sovereign "[can do no wrong](/wiki/Sovereign_immunity "Sovereign immunity")": the monarch cannot be prosecuted in his own courts—judged by himself—for criminal offences under his own laws.[[292]](#cite_note-310) Canada inherited the common law version of Crown immunity from British law.[[293]](#cite_note-Justice-311) However, over time, the scope of said immunity has been steadily reduced by statute law.
