With the passage of relevant legislation through the provincial and federal parliaments, the Crown in its public capacity (that is, lawsuits against the King-in-Council), in all areas of Canada, is now liable in [tort](/wiki/Tort "Tort"), as any normal person would be.[[293]](#cite_note-Justice-311) In international cases, as a sovereign and under established principles of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law"), the King of Canada is not subject to suit in foreign courts without his express consent.[[294]](#cite_note-312)

Within the royal prerogative is also the granting of immunity from prosecution,[[295]](#cite_note-313) mercy, and [pardoning](/wiki/Pardon#Canada "Pardon") offences against the Crown.[[296]](#cite_note-314)[[297]](#cite_note-315) Since 1878, the prerogative of pardon has always been exercised upon the recommendation of ministers.[[298]](#cite_note-316)

### The Crown and Indigenous peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Crown and Indigenous peoples")]

Main article: [Monarchy of Canada and the Indigenous peoples of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada_and_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada and the Indigenous peoples of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Presentation_of_Membertou_Portrait_to_Queen_Elizabeth_II.jpg/250px-Presentation_of_Membertou_Portrait_to_Queen_Elizabeth_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presentation_of_Membertou_Portrait_to_Queen_Elizabeth_II.jpg)

[Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_people "Mi'kmaq people") leaders present a portrait of Grand Chief [Henri Membertou](/wiki/Henri_Membertou "Henri Membertou") to Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") in [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia"), 28 June 2010

Included in Canada's constitution are the various treaties between the Crown and Canada's [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_people_(Canada) "Métis people (Canada)") peoples, who, like the [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") and the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") in New Zealand,[[299]](#cite_note-317) generally view the affiliation as being not between them and the ever-changing Cabinet, but instead with the continuous Crown of Canada, as embodied in the reigning sovereign,[[300]](#cite_note-318) meaning the link between monarch and Indigenous peoples in Canada will theoretically last for "as long as the sun shines, grass grows, and rivers flow."[[301]](#cite_note-FN-319)[[302]](#cite_note-Clarkson-320)

The association stretches back to the [first decisions](/wiki/Timeline_of_colonization_of_North_America "Timeline of colonization of North America") between [North American Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") and European colonialists and, over centuries of interface, [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") were established concerning the monarch and indigenous nations. The only treaties that survived the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") are those in Canada, which date to the beginning of the 18th century. Today, the main guide for relations between the monarchy and Canadian First Nations is King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III")'s *[Royal Proclamation of 1763](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763")*;[[303]](#cite_note-CE-321)[[304]](#cite_note-RC261-322) while not a treaty, it is regarded by First Nations as their [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") or "Indian [bill of rights](/wiki/Bill_of_rights "Bill of rights")",[[304]](#cite_note-RC261-322)[[305]](#cite_note-Valpy-323) as it affirmed native title to their lands and made clear that, though under the [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") of the Crown, the aboriginal bands were autonomous political units in a "nation-to-nation" association with non-native governments,[[306]](#cite_note-CT6FN-324)[[307]](#cite_note-CAP-325) with the monarch as the intermediary.[[308]](#cite_note-Match-326) The agreements with the Crown are administered by [aboriginal law](/wiki/Canadian_Aboriginal_law "Canadian Aboriginal law") and overseen by the [minister of Crown-Indigenous relations](/wiki/Minister_of_Crown%E2%80%93Indigenous_Relations "Minister of Crown–Indigenous Relations").[[309]](#cite_note-327)[[310]](#cite_note-328)

> I have greatly appreciated the opportunity to discuss [...] the vital process of reconciliation in this country—not a one-off act, of course, but an ongoing commitment to healing, respect and understanding [...] with indigenous and non-indigenous peoples across Canada committing to reflect honestly and openly on the past and to forge a new relationship for the future.[[311]](#cite_note-329)

Prince [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles,_Prince_of_Wales "Charles, Prince of Wales"), 2022

The link between the Crown and Indigenous peoples will sometimes be symbolically expressed through ceremony.[[312]](#cite_note-POW-330) Gifts have been frequently exchanged and aboriginal titles have been bestowed upon royal and viceregal figures since the early days of indigenous contact with the Crown.[[302]](#cite_note-Clarkson-320)[[313]](#cite_note-HBC-331)[[314]](#cite_note-332)[[315]](#cite_note-DCHsymb-333)[[316]](#cite_note-334) As far back as 1710, Indigenous leaders have met to discuss treaty business with royal family members or viceroys in private audience and many continue to use their connection to the Crown to further their political aims;[[317]](#cite_note-Coates_2006_13-335) public ceremonies attended by the monarch or another member of the royal family have been employed as a platform on which to present complaints, witnessed by both national and international cameras.[[305]](#cite_note-Valpy-323)[[318]](#cite_note-336)[[319]](#cite_note-337) Following country-wide [protests](/wiki/Idle_No_More "Idle No More"), beginning in 2012, and the close of the [Truth and Reconciliation Commission](/wiki/Truth_and_Reconciliation_Commission_of_Canada "Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada") in 2015, focus turned toward rapprochement between the nations in the nation-to-nation relationship.[[320]](#cite_note-Campbell-338)[[321]](#cite_note-339)[[322]](#cite_note-G&M-340)[[323]](#cite_note-cbcyd-341)[[324]](#cite_note-pow-342)[[325]](#cite_note-343)

#### Hereditary chiefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Hereditary chiefs")]

Main article: [Hereditary chiefs in Canada](/wiki/Hereditary_chiefs_in_Canada "Hereditary chiefs in Canada")

See also: [History of monarchy in Canada § Pre-colonial](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_Canada#Pre-colonial "History of monarchy in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Ho_Nee_Yeath_Taw_No_Row.jpg/250px-Ho_Nee_Yeath_Taw_No_Row.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ho_Nee_Yeath_Taw_No_Row.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/John_Simon_-_Portraits_of_Four_Indian_Kings_of_Canada%2C_Etow_Oh_Koam%2C_King_of_the_River_Nation_-_B2001.2.1507_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg/250px-John_Simon_-_Portraits_of_Four_Indian_Kings_of_Canada%2C_Etow_Oh_Koam%2C_King_of_the_River_Nation_-_B2001.2.1507_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Simon_-_Portraits_of_Four_Indian_Kings_of_Canada,_Etow_Oh_Koam,_King_of_the_River_Nation_-_B2001.2.1507_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/345Chief_Sa_Ga_Yeath_Qua_Pieth_Tow_by_John_Verelst%2C_1710._Archives_of_Canada..jpg/250px-345Chief_Sa_Ga_Yeath_Qua_Pieth_Tow_by_John_Verelst%2C_1710._Archives_of_Canada..jpg)](/wiki/File:345Chief_Sa_Ga_Yeath_Qua_Pieth_Tow_by_John_Verelst,_1710._Archives_of_Canada..jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Tejonihokarawa_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Tejonihokarawa_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tejonihokarawa_(cropped).jpg)

Portraits of the [Four Mohawk Kings](/wiki/Four_Mohawk_Kings "Four Mohawk Kings"), painted in 1710, during their visit with [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain")

The hereditary chiefs are leaders within [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") who represent different houses or clans and whose chieftaincies are passed down intergenerationally; most First Nations have a hereditary system.[[326]](#cite_note-Todd-344) The positions are rooted in traditional models of [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") governance that predate the [colonization of Canada](/wiki/Colonization_of_Canada "Colonization of Canada")[[327]](#cite_note-Joseph-345)[[328]](#cite_note-Abedi-346) and are organized in a fashion similar to the [occidental](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") idea of monarchy.[[326]](#cite_note-Todd-344)[[329]](#cite_note-347)[[330]](#cite_note-348)[[331]](#cite_note-349)[[332]](#cite_note-Guichon-350) Indeed, early European explorers often considered territories belonging to different aboriginal groups to be kingdoms—such as along the north shore of the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"), between the Trinity River and the [Isle-aux-Coudres](/wiki/L%27Isle-aux-Coudres,_Quebec "L'Isle-aux-Coudres, Quebec"), and the neighbouring "kingdom of Canada", which stretched west to the [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal")[[333]](#cite_note-351)—and the leaders of these communities were referred to as kings,[[317]](#cite_note-Coates_2006_13-335) particularly those chosen through heredity.[[334]](#cite_note-352)[[335]](#cite_note-353)

Today, the hereditary chiefs are not sovereign; according to the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada"), the Crown holds sovereignty over the whole of Canada, including reservation and traditional lands.[[336]](#cite_note-SCC1-354)[[337]](#cite_note-SCC2-355)[[338]](#cite_note-SCC3-356) However, by some interpretations of case law from the same court, the chiefs have jurisdiction over [traditional territories](/wiki/List_of_traditional_territories_of_the_indigenous_peoples_of_North_America "List of traditional territories of the indigenous peoples of North America") that fall outside of [band](/wiki/Band_government "Band government")-controlled [reservation land](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve"),[[339]](#cite_note-357)[[340]](#cite_note-358) beyond the elected band councils established by the *[Indian Act](/wiki/Indian_Act "Indian Act")*.[[341]](#cite_note-359)[[342]](#cite_note-360) Although recognized by, and accountable to, the federal Crown-in-Council (the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada")), band chiefs do not hold the cultural authority of hereditary chiefs, who often serve as knowledge-keepers, responsible for the upholding of a First Nation's [traditional customs, legal systems, and cultural practices](/wiki/Traditional_knowledge "Traditional knowledge").[[327]](#cite_note-Joseph-345)[[343]](#cite_note-361)[[344]](#cite_note-362) When serving as Lieutenant Governor of British Columbia, [Judith Guichon](/wiki/Judith_Guichon "Judith Guichon") postulated that the role of hereditary chiefs mirrored that of Canada's constitutional monarch, being the representative of "sober second thought and wisdom, not the next political cycle; but, rather, enduring truths and the evolution of our nation through generations."[[332]](#cite_note-Guichon-350) For these reasons, the Crown maintains formal relations with Canada's hereditary chiefs, including on matters relating to treaty rights and obligations.[[345]](#cite_note-363)

## Cultural role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cultural role")]

### Royal presence and duties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Royal presence and duties")]

Further information: [Royal tours of Canada](/wiki/Royal_tours_of_Canada "Royal tours of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Prince_of_Wales_lays_last_stone_of_Victoria_Bridge.jpg/250px-Prince_of_Wales_lays_last_stone_of_Victoria_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_of_Wales_lays_last_stone_of_Victoria_Bridge.jpg)

[Prince Albert Edward, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII"), lays the last stone for the [Victoria Bridge](/wiki/Victoria_Bridge_(Montreal) "Victoria Bridge (Montreal)") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") during his 1860 royal tour

Members of the royal family have been present in Canada since the late 18th century, their reasons including participating in military manoeuvres, serving as the federal viceroy, or undertaking official royal tours, which "reinforce [the] country's collective heritage".[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) At least one royal tour has been conducted every year between 1957 and 2018.[[347]](#cite_note-365)

The "welfare and service" function of the monarchy is regarded as an important part of the modern monarchy's role and demonstrates a significant change to the institution in recent generations, from a heavily ceremonialized, imperial crown to a "more demotic and visible" head of state "interacting with the general population far beyond confined court circles."[[348]](#cite_note-366) As such, a prominent feature of tours are royal walkabouts; a tradition initiated in 1939 by [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") when she was in Ottawa and broke from the royal party to speak directly to gathered veterans.[[349]](#cite_note-Benoit-367)[[350]](#cite_note-368) Usually important milestones, anniversaries, or celebrations of [Canadian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada") will warrant the presence of the monarch,[[349]](#cite_note-Benoit-367) while other members of the royal family will be asked to participate in lesser occasions. A [household](#Federal_residences_and_royal_household) to assist and tend to the monarch forms part of the royal party.

Official duties involve the sovereign [representing the Canadian state at home or abroad](/wiki/List_of_state_and_official_visits_by_Canada "List of state and official visits by Canada"), or her relations as members of the [royal family](#Royal_family_and_house) participating in government organized ceremonies either in Canada or elsewhere;[[n 19]](#cite_note-Duties-385)[[367]](#cite_note-RT2010-386)[[368]](#cite_note-MJ-387)[[369]](#cite_note-388)[[370]](#cite_note-389) sometimes these individuals are employed in asserting Canada's sovereignty over its territories.[[n 20]](#cite_note-Arctic-391) The advice of the Canadian Cabinet is the impetus for royal participation in any Canadian event, though, at present, the Chief of Protocol and his staff in the [Department of Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Department_of_Canadian_Heritage "Department of Canadian Heritage") are, as part of the State Ceremonial and Canadian Symbols Program,[[372]](#cite_note-392)[[373]](#cite_note-393) responsible for orchestrating any official events in or for Canada that involve the royal family.[[374]](#cite_note-Hansard62-394)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Prince_Philip_as_Colonel-in-Chief_of_the_Royal_Canadian_Regiment.jpg/250px-Prince_Philip_as_Colonel-in-Chief_of_the_Royal_Canadian_Regiment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_Philip_as_Colonel-in-Chief_of_the_Royal_Canadian_Regiment.jpg)

[Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") with the [Royal Canadian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Regiment "Royal Canadian Regiment") as their [colonel-in-chief](/wiki/Colonel-in-chief "Colonel-in-chief"), April 2013

Conversely, unofficial duties are performed by royal family members for Canadian organizations of which they may be [patrons](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"), through their attendance at charity events, visiting with members of the Canadian Forces as colonel-in-chief, or marking certain key anniversaries.[[367]](#cite_note-RT2010-386)[[368]](#cite_note-MJ-387) The invitation and expenses associated with these undertakings are usually borne by the associated organization.[[367]](#cite_note-RT2010-386) In 2005, members of the royal family were present at a total of 76 Canadian engagements, as well as several more through 2006 and 2007.[[375]](#cite_note-395) In the period between 2019 and 2022, they carried out 53 engagements, the number reduced, and all through the latter year and a half being virtual, because of restrictions in place during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Canada "COVID-19 pandemic in Canada").[[376]](#cite_note-396) The various viceroys took part in 4,023 engagements through 2019 and 2020, both in-person and virtually.[[377]](#cite_note-397)

Apart from Canada, the King and other members of the royal family regularly perform public duties in the other 14 Commonwealth realms in which the King is head of state. This situation, however, can mean the monarch and members of the royal family will be promoting one nation and not another; a situation that has been met with criticism.[[n 21]](#cite_note-Sharp-399)

### Symbols, associations, and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Symbols, associations, and awards")]

Main articles: [Canadian royal symbols](/wiki/Canadian_royal_symbols "Canadian royal symbols") and [Monarchy of Canada and the Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada_and_the_Canadian_Armed_Forces "Monarchy of Canada and the Canadian Armed Forces")

The main symbol of the monarchy is the sovereign himself,[[163]](#cite_note-Tidridge16-174) described as "the personal expression of the Crown in Canada,"[[379]](#cite_note-400) and his image is thus used to signify Canadian sovereignty and government authority—his image, for instance, appearing on [currency](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar"), and his portrait in government buildings.[[212]](#cite_note-CIC29-229) The sovereign is further both mentioned in and the subject of songs, [loyal toasts](/wiki/Loyal_toast "Loyal toast"), and salutes.[[380]](#cite_note-401) A [royal cypher](/wiki/Royal_cypher "Royal cypher"), appearing on buildings and official [seals](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)"), or a crown, seen on provincial and [national coats of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_Arms_of_Canada "Coat of Arms of Canada"), as well as [police](/wiki/Police "Police") force and [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") regimental and maritime badges and rank insignia, is also used to illustrate the monarchy as the locus of authority,[[381]](#cite_note-DCH2-402) the latter without referring to any specific monarch.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Queen%27s_Park.JPG/250px-Queen%27s_Park.JPG)](/wiki/File:Queen%27s_Park.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9f/Sovereign%27s_Medal_for_Volunteer_Medal_and_Pin.jpg/120px-Sovereign%27s_Medal_for_Volunteer_Medal_and_Pin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sovereign%27s_Medal_for_Volunteer_Medal_and_Pin.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/King%27s_College_royal_charter_1827_leaf1.jpg/250px-King%27s_College_royal_charter_1827_leaf1.jpg)](/wiki/File:King%27s_College_royal_charter_1827_leaf1.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Flag_of_the_Canadian_Forces.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Canadian_Forces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Canadian_Forces.svg)

(Clockwise from top) equestrian statue of King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII"), [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_(Toronto) "Queen's Park (Toronto)"), [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"); [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV")'s [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") establishing the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"); flag of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces"), with a [St. Edward's Crown](/wiki/St._Edward%27s_Crown "St. Edward's Crown") atop the forces' emblem; [Sovereign's Medal for Volunteers](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Medal_for_Volunteers "Sovereign's Medal for Volunteers"), with the effigy of Elizabeth II wearing a [snowflake diadem](/wiki/Canadian_royal_symbols#Snowflake_Diadem "Canadian royal symbols")

Since the days of King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"),[[382]](#cite_note-McCreery1-403) the monarch is the [fount](/wiki/Fount_of_honour "Fount of honour") of all [honours in Canada](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Canada "Orders, decorations, and medals of Canada") and the orders,[[382]](#cite_note-McCreery1-403)[[383]](#cite_note-404) decorations, and medals form "an integral element of the Crown."[[382]](#cite_note-McCreery1-403) Hence, the insignia and medallions for these awards bear a crown, cypher or portrait of the monarch. Similarly, [the country's heraldic authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority") was created by Queen Elizabeth II and, operating under the authority of the governor general, grants new [coats of arms](/wiki/Coats_of_arms "Coats of arms"), [flags](/wiki/Flag "Flag"), and [badges](/wiki/Heraldic_badge "Heraldic badge") in Canada. Use of the royal crown in such symbols is a gift from the monarch showing royal support or association and requires his approval before being added.[[381]](#cite_note-DCH2-402)[[384]](#cite_note-405)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Canadian_Royal_Cypher_of_Charles_III.svg/120px-Canadian_Royal_Cypher_of_Charles_III.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Canadian_Royal_Cypher_of_Charles_III.svg)

King Charles III's Cypher in Canada

Members of the royal family also act as ceremonial [colonels-in-chief](/wiki/Colonel-in-Chief "Colonel-in-Chief"), [commodores-in-chief](/wiki/Commodore-in-Chief "Commodore-in-Chief"), [captains-general](/wiki/Captain_general "Captain general"), [air commodores-in-chief](/wiki/Air_Commodore-in-Chief "Air Commodore-in-Chief"), [generals](/wiki/General_(Canada) "General (Canada)"), and [admirals](/wiki/Admiral_(Canada) "Admiral (Canada)") of various elements of the Canadian Forces, reflecting [the Crown's relationship with the country's military](/wiki/The_Canadian_Crown_and_the_Canadian_Forces "The Canadian Crown and the Canadian Forces") through participation in events both at home and abroad.[[n 22]](#cite_note-CF-406) The monarch also serves as the Commissioner-in-Chief, and Prince Edward and Princess Anne as Honorary Deputy Commissioners, of the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police").[[385]](#cite_note-407)

A number of Canadian civilian organizations have association with the monarchy, either through their being founded via a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter#Canada "Royal charter"), having been granted [the right to use the prefix *royal* before their name](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_organizations_with_royal_prefix "List of Canadian organizations with royal prefix"), or because at least one member of the royal family serves as a [patron](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"). In addition to [The Prince's Trust Canada](/wiki/The_Prince%27s_Trust#Subsidiaries "The Prince's Trust"), established by Charles III when Prince of Wales, some other charities and volunteer organizations have also been founded as gifts to, or in honour of, some of Canada's monarchs or members of the royal family, such as the [Victorian Order of Nurses](/wiki/Victorian_Order_of_Nurses "Victorian Order of Nurses"), a gift to Queen Victoria for her [Diamond Jubilee](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee#Commonwealth_Realms/British_Empire "Diamond Jubilee") in 1897; the Canadian Cancer Fund, set up in honour of King George V's [Silver Jubilee](/wiki/Silver_Jubilee#Silver_Jubilees_in_the_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Realms "Silver Jubilee") in 1935; and the Queen Elizabeth II Fund to Aid in Research on the Diseases of Children. A number of [awards in Canada](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_awards "List of Canadian awards") are likewise issued in the name of previous or present members of the royal family. Further, organizations will give commemorative gifts to members of the royal family to mark a visit or other important occasion. All Canadian coins bear the image of the monarch reigning at the time of the coin's production, with an inscription, *[Dei gratia Rex](/wiki/Dei_gratia_Rex "Dei gratia Rex")* (often abbreviated to *DG Rex*), a Latin phrase translated to English as, "[by the grace of God](/wiki/By_the_grace_of_God "By the grace of God"), king".[[386]](#cite_note-408) During the reign of a female monarch, *rex* is replaced with *regina*, which is Latin for 'queen'.

Throughout the 1970s, symbols of the monarch and monarchy were slowly removed from the public eye. For instance, the Queen's portrait was seen less in [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(government_funded) "Public school (government funded)") and the Royal Mail became [Canada Post](/wiki/Canada_Post "Canada Post"). Smith attributed this to the attitude the government of the day held toward Canada's past;[[387]](#cite_note-409) though, it never raised the policy in public or during any of the constitutional conferences held that decade.[[120]](#cite_note-Toporoski1998-130) Andrew Heard argued, however, that dispensing with such symbols was necessary to facilitate the simultaneous increasing embrace of the monarch as Queen of Canada.[[388]](#cite_note-410) Emblems such as the Royal Coat of Arms remained, however, and others, such as the [monarch's royal standard](/wiki/Royal_standards_of_Canada "Royal standards of Canada"), were created. With the later developments of the [governor general's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_governor_general_of_Canada "Flag of the governor general of Canada"), foundation of the Canadian Heraldic Authority, royal standards for other members of the royal family, and the like, Canada, along with New Zealand, is one of the two [realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") that have "paid the greatest attention to the nationalization of the visual symbols of the monarchy."[[389]](#cite_note-411)

### Significance to Canadian identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Significance to Canadian identity")]

Main article: [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity")

In his 2018 book, *The Canadian Kingdom: 150 Years of Constitutional Monarchy*, Jackson wrote that "the Canadian manifestation of the monarchy is not only historical and constitutional, it is political, cultural, and social, reflecting, and contributing to, change and evolution in Canada's governance, autonomy, and identity."[[53]](#cite_note-Jackson14-55) Since at least the 1930s,[[390]](#cite_note-412) supporters of the Crown have held the opinion that the monarch is a unifying focal point for the nation's "historic consciousness"—the country's heritage being "unquestionably linked with the history of monarchy"[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364)—and Canadian [patriotism](/wiki/Patriotism "Patriotism"), traditions, and shared values,[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) "around which coheres the nation's sense of a continuing personality".[[391]](#cite_note-413) This infusion of monarchy into Canadian governance and society helps strengthen [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity")[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) and distinguish it from American identity,[[392]](#cite_note-414) a difference that has existed since at least 1864, when it was a factor in the Fathers of [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") choosing to keep constitutional monarchy for the new country in 1866.[[393]](#cite_note-Tidridge20-415) Former Governor General [Vincent Massey](/wiki/Vincent_Massey "Vincent Massey") articulated in 1967 that the monarchy "stands for qualities and institutions which mean Canada to every one of us and which, for all our differences and all our variety, have kept Canada Canadian."[[394]](#cite_note-416)

> I want the Crown in Canada to represent everything that is best and most admired in the Canadian ideal. I will continue to do my best to make it so during my lifetime.[[395]](#cite_note-417)

Elizabeth II, 1973

But, Canadians were, through the late 1960s to the 2000s, encouraged by federal and provincial governments to "neglect, ignore, forget, reject, debase, suppress, even hate, and certainly treat as foreign what their parents and grandparents, whether spiritual or blood, regarded as the basis of Canadian nationhood, autonomy, and history", including the monarchy[[396]](#cite_note-418) resulting in a disconnect between the Canadian populace and their monarch.[[393]](#cite_note-Tidridge20-415) Former Governor General [Roland Michener](/wiki/Roland_Michener "Roland Michener") said in 1970 that anti-monarchists claimed the Canadian Crown is foreign and incompatible with Canada's multicultural society,[[258]](#cite_note-ECF-275) which the government promoted as a Canadian identifier, and [Lawrence Martin](/wiki/Lawrence_Martin_(journalist) "Lawrence Martin (journalist)") called in 2007 for Canada to become a republic in order to "re-brand the nation".[[397]](#cite_note-419) However, Michener also stated, "[the monarchy] is our own by inheritance and choice, and contributes much to our distinctive Canadian identity and our chances of independent survival amongst the republics of North and South America."[[258]](#cite_note-ECF-275) Journalist Christina Blizzard emphasized in 2009 that the monarchy "made [Canada] a haven of peace and justice for immigrants from around the world",[[398]](#cite_note-420) while [Michael Valpy](/wiki/Michael_Valpy "Michael Valpy") contended in 2009 that the Crown's nature permitted non-conformity amongst its subjects, thereby opening the door to multiculturalism and pluralism.[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40) Johnston described the Crown as providing "space for our values and beliefs as Canadians."[[164]](#cite_note-Johnstonxi-175)

### In media and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: In media and popular culture")]

Further information: [Canadian royal symbols § Artworks](/wiki/Canadian_royal_symbols#Artworks "Canadian royal symbols"), and [Personality and image of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Personality_and_image_of_Elizabeth_II "Personality and image of Elizabeth II")

#### Painting and sculpture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Painting and sculpture")]

Aside from official artworks, such as monuments and portraits commissioned by government bodies, Canadian painters have, by their own volition or for private organizations, created more expressive, informal depictions of Canada's monarchs and other members of the royal family, ranging from [fine art](/wiki/Fine_art "Fine art") to irreverent [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti"). For example, the English-Canadian artist [Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith](/wiki/Frederic_Marlett_Bell-Smith "Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith") produced *[The Artist Painting Queen Victoria](/wiki/Frederic_Marlett_Bell-Smith#Painting_Queen_Victoria "Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith")* in 1895, which now resides at the [National Gallery of Canada](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Canada "National Gallery of Canada"). At [Library and Archives Canada](/wiki/Library_and_Archives_Canada "Library and Archives Canada") is the painting *The Unveiling of the National War Memorial*, capturing [the dedication](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada)#Dedication_and_onward "National War Memorial (Canada)") of [the monument](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada) "National War Memorial (Canada)"), in Ottawa, by King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") and [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") in 1939; though, the artist is unknown.[[399]](#cite_note-421)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Painting_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II_by_Lorena_Ziraldo_2014.jpg/250px-Painting_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II_by_Lorena_Ziraldo_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Painting_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II_by_Lorena_Ziraldo_2014.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Victoria_Day_2013.jpg/250px-Victoria_Day_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Day_2013.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Charles_-_RLWMV_%285034821673%29.jpg/250px-Charles_-_RLWMV_%285034821673%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_-_RLWMV_(5034821673).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Frederic_M._Bell-Smith_-_The_Artist_Painting_Queen_Victoria.jpg/250px-Frederic_M._Bell-Smith_-_The_Artist_Painting_Queen_Victoria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederic_M._Bell-Smith_-_The_Artist_Painting_Queen_Victoria.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Ottawa-1939.jpg/250px-Ottawa-1939.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottawa-1939.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Queen_Vic_%28218234903%29.jpeg/120px-Queen_Vic_%28218234903%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Vic_(218234903).jpeg)

(Clockwise from top) portrait of [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") by [Lorena Ziraldo](/wiki/Lorena_Ziraldo "Lorena Ziraldo"), 2014; wax figure of Prince Charles (now Charles III) at the Royal London Wax Museum, Victoria; [Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith](/wiki/Frederic_Marlett_Bell-Smith "Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith")'s *[The Artist Painting Queen Victoria](/wiki/Frederic_Marlett_Bell-Smith#Painting_Queen_Victoria "Frederic Marlett Bell-Smith")*, 1895; [pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art") portrait of Victoria, Toronto; *The Unveiling of the National War Memorial*, 1939; irreverent graffiti in Montreal depicting Elizabeth II

[Hilton Hassell](/w/index.php?title=Hilton_Hassell&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hilton Hassell (page does not exist)") depicted Princess Elizabeth (later Queen Elizabeth II) [square dancing](/wiki/Square_dance "Square dance") at Rideau Hall in 1951 and a portrait of Elizabeth II by [Lorena Ziraldo](/wiki/Lorena_Ziraldo "Lorena Ziraldo"), of [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), was featured in the *[Hill Times](/wiki/Hill_Times "Hill Times")* and *[Ottawa Citizen](/wiki/Ottawa_Citizen "Ottawa Citizen")*.

[Charles Pachter](/wiki/Charles_Pachter "Charles Pachter"), from [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), fashioned the painting *Noblesse Oblige* in 1972, which shows Queen Elizabeth II, in her Guards Regiment uniform and saluting, as she did during [Trooping the Colour](/wiki/Trooping_the_Colour "Trooping the Colour") ceremonies, except atop a moose instead of her horse, [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_(horse) "Burmese (horse)"). Despite great controversy when it was first exhibited,[[400]](#cite_note-Pachter-422) it "has become a Canadian cultural image; the people's image".[[400]](#cite_note-Pachter-422)[[401]](#cite_note-Knelman-423) Pachter, subsequently made numerous variations on the theme,[[402]](#cite_note-424) including *Queen & Moose* (1973)[[403]](#cite_note-425) and *The Queen on a Moose* (1988).[[404]](#cite_note-426) The artist said, "there was an amazing symmetry of putting the sovereign of her northern realm (Canada) on an animal who is the 'monarch of the north, awkward but majestic'".[[400]](#cite_note-Pachter-422) Pachter made similar pieces showing Elizabeth's son, Prince Charles (now King Charles III) and his wife, [Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla"), standing alongside a moose[[401]](#cite_note-Knelman-423) and Charles's son, [Prince William](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"), and his wife, [Catherine](/wiki/Catherine,_Princess_of_Wales "Catherine, Princess of Wales"), with Canadian wildlife, such as a moose and a squirrel.[[405]](#cite_note-427) For [Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), Pachter created a series of fake [postage stamps](/wiki/Postage_stamp "Postage stamp") using all his paintings that include members of the royal family,[[400]](#cite_note-Pachter-422) which he called "my branded images
for Canada."[[406]](#cite_note-UC-428) Some were featured on accessory items sold at the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company").[[406]](#cite_note-UC-428)

Portraits of Elizabeth II hung in several hockey arenas across Canada after her accession in 1952. One was in place in [Maple Leaf Gardens](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_Gardens "Maple Leaf Gardens") until the early 1970s, when owner [Harold Ballard](/wiki/Harold_Ballard "Harold Ballard") had it removed to construct more seating, stating, "if people want to see pictures of the Queen, they can go to an art gallery."[[407]](#cite_note-puckstruck-429) [Three large portraits of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Monarchy_in_Manitoba#Winnipeg_Arena_portraits_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II "Monarchy in Manitoba") were created for [Winnipeg Arena](/wiki/Winnipeg_Arena "Winnipeg Arena"), on display there from the building's opening in 1955 to 1999.[[407]](#cite_note-puckstruck-429)[[408]](#cite_note-430)[[409]](#cite_note-431)[[410]](#cite_note-432)

At the time of [the sesquicentennial of Confederation](/wiki/150th_anniversary_of_Canada "150th anniversary of Canada") in 2017, [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island")-based[[411]](#cite_note-433) artist [Timothy Hoey](/w/index.php?title=Timothy_Hoey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Timothy Hoey (page does not exist)") created a "Canada 150" version of his decade-long "O Canada" project, painting 150 Canadian icons in [acrylic paint](/wiki/Acrylic_paint "Acrylic paint") on 20.3 by 25.4 centimetre (eight by 10 inch) boards.[[412]](#cite_note-CBCHoey-434)[[413]](#cite_note-TC-435) Among them are numerous depictions of Queen Elizabeth II with other Canadian icons, such as [beavers](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver"), [Cheezies](/wiki/Cheezies "Cheezies"), the [Grey Cup](/wiki/Grey_Cup "Grey Cup"),[[412]](#cite_note-CBCHoey-434) the [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup"),[[413]](#cite_note-TC-435) a bottle of beer (*O Canada Liz Enjoying Some Wobbly-Pops*),[[414]](#cite_note-EG-436) [Rush](/wiki/Rush_(band) "Rush (band)") (*O Canada [Closer to the Heart](/wiki/Closer_to_the_Heart "Closer to the Heart")*), the [Hudson's Bay point blanket](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_point_blanket "Hudson's Bay point blanket"),[[414]](#cite_note-EG-436) the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway"), a birch [canoe](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe"), a [buckskin](/wiki/Buckskin_(leather) "Buckskin (leather)") jacket, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police uniform, a [Montreal Canadiens](/wiki/Montreal_Canadiens "Montreal Canadiens") [hockey sweater](/wiki/Hockey_jersey "Hockey jersey"), and so on.[[412]](#cite_note-CBCHoey-434) Hoey had previously painted Elizabeth, in formal attire and tiara, holding a hockey stick in front of a Hudson's Bay point blanket; the work titled *O-Canada Liz*.[[415]](#cite_note-437) In 2021, he depicted the Queen in a decorative hat, uniform of the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") from the 1978–1979 season, and full [goaltender](/wiki/Goaltender "Goaltender") equipment.[[416]](#cite_note-438)

The also exist [wax sculptures](/wiki/Wax_sculpture "Wax sculpture") of Queen Elizabeth II in private museums, such as the Royal London [Wax Museum](/wiki/Wax_Museum "Wax Museum") in [Victoria, British Columbia](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), and the [Wax Museum of History](/wiki/Clifton_Hill,_Niagara_Falls#Wax_museums "Clifton Hill, Niagara Falls") in [Niagara Falls, Ontario](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_Ontario "Niagara Falls, Ontario").[[417]](#cite_note-439)

#### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Television")]

The television series *[Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall_(TV_series) "Rideau Hall (TV series)")*, starring [Bette MacDonald](/wiki/Bette_MacDonald "Bette MacDonald"), was produced by the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") and aired for one season in 2002. Its premise was a brash, [one-hit wonder](/wiki/One-hit_wonder "One-hit wonder") [disco](/wiki/Disco_(music) "Disco (music)") artist being appointed governor general on the advice of a republican prime minister.[[418]](#cite_note-440)[[419]](#cite_note-441)

| External videos | |
| --- | --- |
| [video icon](/wiki/File:Nuvola_apps_kaboodle.svg) [Scott Thompson as The Queen](https://vimeo.com/748072647) Source: [Broadway Video](/wiki/Broadway_Video "Broadway Video"). | |

Canadian comedian [Scott Thompson](/wiki/Scott_Thompson_(comedian) "Scott Thompson (comedian)") regularly played a parody of Queen Elizabeth II in a Canadian context on the [sketch comedy](/wiki/Sketch_comedy "Sketch comedy") television show *[The Kids in the Hall](/wiki/The_Kids_in_the_Hall_(TV_series) "The Kids in the Hall (TV series)")*,[[420]](#cite_note-442) as well as in other productions, such as *The Queen's Toast: A Royal Wedding Special*[[421]](#cite_note-443) and *[Conan](/wiki/Conan_(talk_show) "Conan (talk show)")*. Thompson also voiced a portrayal of Queen Elizabeth II in Canada in the animated television show *[Fugget About It](/wiki/Fugget_About_It "Fugget About It")*, in the episode "Royally Screwed".[[422]](#cite_note-444)

The Canadian monarchy was [parodied](/wiki/Parody "Parody") in "[Royal Pudding](/wiki/Royal_Pudding "Royal Pudding")", the third [episode](/wiki/Episode "Episode") of the [15th season](/wiki/South_Park_(season_15) "South Park (season 15)") of the [animated](/wiki/Animation "Animation") television show *[South Park](/wiki/South_Park "South Park")*, which first aired on 11 May 2011.[[423]](#cite_note-445) The opening focuses on a spoof of the [wedding of Prince William and Catherine Middleton](/wiki/Wedding_of_Prince_William_and_Catherine_Middleton "Wedding of Prince William and Catherine Middleton"),[[424]](#cite_note-446)[[425]](#cite_note-447) featuring caricatures of Queen Elizabeth II; Prince William, Prince of Wales; and Catherine, Princess of Wales. Specific mention is made of "the Queen of Canada" and "the Canadian royal family".[[426]](#cite_note-448) The show subsequently, in the second episode of the [26th season](/wiki/South_Park_(season_26) "South Park (season 26)"), "[The Worldwide Privacy Tour](/wiki/The_Worldwide_Privacy_Tour "The Worldwide Privacy Tour")", parodied [the Duke](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex") and [Duchess of Sussex](/wiki/Meghan,_Duchess_of_Sussex "Meghan, Duchess of Sussex") as a prince of Canada and "the wife", who, after hostile treatment at the [funeral of the late Queen of Canada](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Queen Elizabeth II"), go on national television and a world tour demanding people and the media not pay attention to them and [branding](/wiki/Personal_branding "Personal branding") themselves as victims.[[427]](#cite_note-449)

## Royal family and house

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Royal family and house")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Royal_motorcade_%288075978363%29.jpg/250px-Royal_motorcade_%288075978363%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_motorcade_(8075978363).jpg)

Members of the royal family standing in convertibles during the opening of [1978 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games") in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"); Elizabeth II (front, left), Prince Philip (front, right), [Prince Andrew](/wiki/Prince_Andrew "Prince Andrew") (rear, left), and [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh") (rear, right)

The Canadian royal family is the group of people who are comparatively closely related to the country's monarch and,[[428]](#cite_note-450) as such, belong to the [House of Windsor](/wiki/House_of_Windsor "House of Windsor") and owe their allegiance specifically to the reigning king or queen of Canada.[[429]](#cite_note-Noonan-451) There is no legal definition of who is or is not a member of the royal family; though, the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada")'s website lists "working members of the royal family".[[430]](#cite_note-Memb-452)

Unlike in the United Kingdom, the monarch is the only member of the royal family with a [title established through Canadian law](/wiki/Style_and_title_of_the_Canadian_sovereign "Style and title of the Canadian sovereign") and is styled by convention as *His/Her Majesty*,[[431]](#cite_note-Style-453) as would be a [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort"). Otherwise, the remaining family members are, as a [courtesy](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title"), styled and titled as they are in the UK,[[431]](#cite_note-Style-453) according to [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") issued there,[[432]](#cite_note-454)[[433]](#cite_note-455) with additional French translations.[[434]](#cite_note-456)

Those in the royal family are distant relations of the [Belgian](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium#Royal_family "Monarchy of Belgium"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_royal_family "Danish royal family"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_royal_family "Greek royal family"), [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_royal_family "Norwegian royal family"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_royal_family "Spanish royal family"), and [Swedish royal families](/wiki/Swedish_royal_family "Swedish royal family") and,[[435]](#cite_note-Bousfield22-457) given the shared nature of the Canadian monarch, are also members of the [British royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family"). While Canadian and foreign media often refer to them as the "British royal family",[[436]](#cite_note-458)[[437]](#cite_note-459) the Canadian government considers it inappropriate, as they are family members of the Canadian monarch.[[438]](#cite_note-SaskProtocol-460) Further, in addition to the few Canadian citizens in the royal family,[[n 23]](#cite_note-Citizen-465) the sovereign is considered Canadian,[[443]](#cite_note-466)[[444]](#cite_note-467)[[445]](#cite_note-Proudfoot-468) and those among his relations who do not meet the requirements of [Canadian citizenship law](/wiki/Canadian_nationality_law "Canadian nationality law") are considered Canadian, which entitles them to [Canadian consular assistance](/wiki/Diplomatic_missions_of_Canada "Diplomatic missions of Canada") and the protection of the King's [armed forces of Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") when they are in need of protection or aid outside of the Commonwealth realms,[[429]](#cite_note-Noonan-451) as well as, since 2013, substantive appointment to the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") and [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)").[[446]](#cite_note-469)[[447]](#cite_note-470)[[448]](#cite_note-471) Beyond formalities, members of the royal family have, on occasion, been said by the media and non-governmental organizations to be Canadian,[[n 24]](#cite_note-CanadianRF-476) have declared themselves to be Canadian,[[n 25]](#cite_note-Canadians-482) and some past members have lived in Canada for extended periods as viceroy or for other reasons.[[n 26]](#cite_note-RF2-488)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Edward_VIII_unveils_the_figure_of_Canada_on_the_Vimy_Ridge_Memorial.jpg/250px-Edward_VIII_unveils_the_figure_of_Canada_on_the_Vimy_Ridge_Memorial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_VIII_unveils_the_figure_of_Canada_on_the_Vimy_Ridge_Memorial.jpg)

King [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") unveiling the figure *Canada Bereft* on the [Canadian National Vimy Memorial](/wiki/Canadian_National_Vimy_Memorial "Canadian National Vimy Memorial") in July 1936

According to the Canadian Royal Heritage Trust, [Prince Edward Augustus, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Augustus,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward Augustus, Duke of Kent and Strathearn")—due to his having lived in Canada between 1791 and 1800 and fathering [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")—is the "ancestor of the modern Canadian royal family".[[460]](#cite_note-Toffoli-485) Nonetheless, the concept of the Canadian royal family did not emerge until after the passage of the Statute of Westminster in 1931, when Canadian officials only began to overtly consider putting the principles of Canada's new status as an independent kingdom into effect.[[463]](#cite_note-Parl-489) Initially, the monarch was the only member of the royal family to carry out public ceremonial duties solely on the advice of Canadian ministers; King [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") became the first to do so when in July 1936 he dedicated the [Canadian National Vimy Memorial](/wiki/Canadian_National_Vimy_Memorial "Canadian National Vimy Memorial") in France.[[n 19]](#cite_note-Duties-385) Over the decades, however, the monarch's children, grandchildren, cousins, and their respective spouses began to also perform functions at the direction of the Canadian Crown-in-Council, representing the monarch within Canada or abroad, in a role specifically as members of the Canadian royal family.[[464]](#cite_note-490)

However, it was not until October 2002 when the term *Canadian royal family* was first used publicly and officially by one of its members: in a speech to the [Nunavut legislature](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Nunavut "Legislative Assembly of Nunavut") at its opening, Queen Elizabeth II stated: "I am proud to be the first member of the Canadian royal family to be greeted in Canada's newest territory."[[465]](#cite_note-491)[[466]](#cite_note-Jackson210-492) Princess Anne used it again when speaking at Rideau Hall in 2014,[[467]](#cite_note-493) as did the now King Charles in Halifax the same year.[[468]](#cite_note-494) Also in 2014, Premier of Saskatchewan [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall") called Prince Edward a member of the Canadian royal family.[[469]](#cite_note-495) By 2011, both Canadian and British media were referring to "Canada's royal family" or the "Canadian royal family".[[470]](#cite_note-496)[[471]](#cite_note-497)[[472]](#cite_note-498)[[473]](#cite_note-499)

While Heard observed in 2018 that no direct legal action has, so far, created a Canadian royal family,[[474]](#cite_note-Heard126-500) he also asserted that the [Canadian Heraldic Authority](/wiki/Canadian_Heraldic_Authority "Canadian Heraldic Authority") creating [uniquely Canadian standards for members of the royal family other than the monarch](/wiki/Royal_standards_of_Canada "Royal standards of Canada") was a symbolic "localization of the royal family";[[475]](#cite_note-Heard115-501) Sean Palmer agreed, stating the banners are a sign the country has taken "'ownership' not only of the Queen of Canada, but of the other members of her family as well" and that doing so was another formal affirmation of the concept of a Canadian royal family "as distinct as the Queen of Canada is from the Queen of the United Kingdom".[[466]](#cite_note-Jackson210-492) Jai Patel and Sally Raudon also noted, in 2019, that the purpose of these heraldic banners was to recognize the owners' roles as members of the Canadian royal family.[[476]](#cite_note-502)

## Federal residences and royal household

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Federal residences and royal household")]

Main article: [Government Houses in Canada](/wiki/Government_Houses_in_Canada "Government Houses in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Ottawa_-_Rideau_Hall.JPG/250px-Ottawa_-_Rideau_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ottawa_-_Rideau_Hall.JPG)

[Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Residence_of_the_Governor_General_%28Quebec%29_01.jpg/250px-Residence_of_the_Governor_General_%28Quebec%29_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Residence_of_the_Governor_General_(Quebec)_01.jpg)

[Citadelle of Quebec](/wiki/Citadelle_of_Quebec "Citadelle of Quebec")

Official residences of the sovereign and their representative, the governor general

Buildings across Canada reserved by the Crown for the use of the monarch and his viceroys are called *[Government House](/wiki/Government_House "Government House")*, but may be customarily known by some specific name. The sovereign's and governor general's [official residences](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") are [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall") in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") and the [Citadelle](/wiki/Citadelle_of_Quebec "Citadelle of Quebec") in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City").[[n 27]](#cite_note-Residence-505)[[488]](#cite_note-515) Each holds pieces from the [Crown Collection](/wiki/Crown_Collection "Crown Collection").[[489]](#cite_note-516) Though neither was used for their intended purpose, [Hatley Castle](/wiki/Hatley_Castle "Hatley Castle") in British Columbia was purchased in 1940 by the federal government for the use of [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") and his family during the Second World War[[490]](#cite_note-517) and the [Emergency Government Headquarters](/wiki/Emergency_Government_Headquarters "Emergency Government Headquarters"), built between 1959 and 1961 at [CFS Carp](/wiki/CFS_Carp "CFS Carp") and decommissioned in 1994, included a residential apartment for the sovereign or governor general in the case of a [nuclear attack](/wiki/Nuclear_attack "Nuclear attack").[[491]](#cite_note-518)

British royalty have also owned homes and land in Canada in a private capacity: [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") owned Bedingfield Ranch, near [Pekisko, Alberta](/wiki/Pekisko,_Alberta "Pekisko, Alberta");[[492]](#cite_note-519) and [Princess Margaret](/wiki/Princess_Margaret,_Countess_of_Snowdon "Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon") owned [Portland Island](/wiki/Portland_Island_(British_Columbia) "Portland Island (British Columbia)"), which was given to her by British Columbia in 1958. She offered it back to the province on permanent loan in 1961, which was accepted in 1966, and the island and surrounding waters eventually became Princess Margaret Marine Park.[[493]](#cite_note-Legacy-520)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/HRH_The_Duke_of_Connaught_at_Mohawk_Church%2C_Brantford_%28HS85-10-26794%29.jpg/250px-HRH_The_Duke_of_Connaught_at_Mohawk_Church%2C_Brantford_%28HS85-10-26794%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HRH_The_Duke_of_Connaught_at_Mohawk_Church,_Brantford_(HS85-10-26794).jpg)

[Prince Arthur](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn") at [Mohawk Chapel](/wiki/Mohawk_Chapel "Mohawk Chapel") in 1913. The sanctuary was designated a [chapel royal](/wiki/Chapel_royal "Chapel royal") in 1904.

In addition to a [maître d'hôtel](/wiki/Ma%C3%AEtre_d%27h%C3%B4tel "Maître d'hôtel"), [chefs](/wiki/Chef "Chef"), [footmen](/wiki/Footmen "Footmen"), [valets](/wiki/Valets "Valets"), dressers, [pages](/wiki/Page_(servant) "Page (servant)"), aides-de-camp (drawn from the junior officers of the armed forces), [equerries](/wiki/Equerries "Equerries"), and others at Rideau Hall,[[494]](#cite_note-521) the King appoints various people to his Canadian [household](/wiki/Household#Historical_households "Household") to assist him in carrying out his official duties on behalf of Canada. Along with the [Canadian secretary to the King](/wiki/Canadian_secretary_to_the_King "Canadian secretary to the King"),[[374]](#cite_note-Hansard62-394) the monarch's entourage includes the [equerry-in-waiting](/wiki/Equerry#Canada "Equerry") to the King, the King's police officer, two [ladies-in-waiting](/wiki/Ladies-in-waiting "Ladies-in-waiting") for the Queen,[[495]](#cite_note-522) the King's honorary physician, the King's honorary dental surgeon, and the King's honorary nursing officer[[496]](#cite_note-523)—the latter three being drawn from the Canadian Forces.[[134]](#cite_note-CRHT2-144) [Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh"), also has a Canadian private secretary and his wife,[[497]](#cite_note-524) [Sophie, Duchess of Edinburgh](/wiki/Sophie,_Duchess_of_Edinburgh "Sophie, Duchess of Edinburgh"), a lady-in-waiting.[[498]](#cite_note-525) [Royal Canadian Air Force VIP aircraft](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force_VIP_aircraft "Royal Canadian Air Force VIP aircraft") are provided by [412 Transport Squadron](/wiki/412_Transport_Squadron "412 Transport Squadron").

There are three [household regiments](/wiki/Household_Division#Canada "Household Division") specifically attached to the royal household—the [Governor General's Foot Guards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Foot_Guards "Governor General's Foot Guards"), [the Governor General's Horse Guards](/wiki/The_Governor_General%27s_Horse_Guards "The Governor General's Horse Guards"), and [the Canadian Grenadier Guards](/wiki/The_Canadian_Grenadier_Guards "The Canadian Grenadier Guards"). There are also three [chapels royal](/wiki/Chapels_royal "Chapels royal"), all in Ontario:[[499]](#cite_note-526) [Mohawk Chapel](/wiki/Mohawk_Chapel "Mohawk Chapel") in [Brantford](/wiki/Brantford,_Ontario "Brantford, Ontario"); [Christ Church Royal Chapel](/wiki/Christ_Church_Royal_Chapel "Christ Church Royal Chapel"), near [Deseronto](/wiki/Deseronto "Deseronto"); and St Catherine's Chapel in [Massey College](/wiki/Massey_College "Massey College"), in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"). Though not a chapel royal, [St Bartholomew's Anglican Church](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Anglican_Church_(Ottawa) "St. Bartholomew's Anglican Church (Ottawa)"), located across MacKay Street from Rideau Hall, is regularly used by governors general and their families and sometimes by the sovereign and other visiting royalty, as well as by staff, their families, and members of the Governor General's Foot Guards, for whom the church serves as a regimental chapel.[[500]](#cite_note-527)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Security")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Royal_Visit_Toronto_2010_5.JPG/250px-Royal_Visit_Toronto_2010_5.JPG)](/wiki/File:Royal_Visit_Toronto_2010_5.JPG)

A Queen's Police Officer, drawn from the [RCMP](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"), with [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Will_and_Kate_in_Prince_Edward_Island_Canada_2011.jpg/250px-Will_and_Kate_in_Prince_Edward_Island_Canada_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Will_and_Kate_in_Prince_Edward_Island_Canada_2011.jpg)

The Duke and Duchess of Cambridge with an RCMP escort, in [Charlottetown](/wiki/Charlottetown "Charlottetown")

The Royal Canadian Mounted Police is tasked with providing security to the sovereign, the governor general (starting from when they are made [governor general-designate](/wiki/-elect "-elect")[[501]](#cite_note-528)), and other members of the royal family; as outlined in the *RCMP Regulations*, the force "has a duty to protect individuals designated by the minister of public safety, including certain members of the royal family when visiting."[[502]](#cite_note-RCMP-529) The RCMP's provision of service is determined based on threat and risk assessment, the seniority of the individual in terms of precedence and.[[n 28]](#cite_note-Sussex-531) for members of the royal family, the nature of the royal tour—i.e. an official tour by the King or on behalf of the King or a working or private visit.[[502]](#cite_note-RCMP-529) The governor general receives round-the-clock security from the Governor General Protection Detail,[[504]](#cite_note-532) part of the [Personal Protection Group](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police#Personal_Protection_Group "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"), based at Rideau Hall.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of monarchy in Canada](/wiki/History_of_monarchy_in_Canada "History of monarchy in Canada")

Further information: [List of Canadian monarchs](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_monarchs "List of Canadian monarchs")

### From colonies to independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: From colonies to independence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Fran%C3%A7ois_Ier_Louvre.jpg/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois_Ier_Louvre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois_Ier_Louvre.jpg)

King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") established colonies in [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") in 1534.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Queen_Victoria%2C_c.1870._%287936242480%29.jpg/250px-Queen_Victoria%2C_c.1870._%287936242480%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Victoria,_c.1870._(7936242480).jpg)

[Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in 1870, three years after her royal assent to the *[British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*, creating the [Canadian federation](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism")

The Canadian monarchy can trace its ancestral lineage back to the [kings of the Angles](/wiki/King_of_the_Angles "King of the Angles") and the early [Scottish kings](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_monarchs "List of Scottish monarchs") and through the centuries since the claims of King [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") in 1497 and King [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") in 1534; both being [blood relatives](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity") of the current Canadian monarch. Former Prime Minister [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") said of the Crown that it "links us all together with the majestic past that takes us back to the [Tudors](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor"), the [Plantagenets](/wiki/House_of_Plantagenet "House of Plantagenet"), [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), *[habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus")*, [petition of rights](/wiki/Petition_of_Right "Petition of Right"), and English [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law")."[[505]](#cite_note-533) Though the first French and British colonizers of Canada interpreted the hereditary nature of some indigenous North American chieftainships as a form of monarchy,[[506]](#cite_note-534)[[507]](#cite_note-535)[[508]](#cite_note-536) it is generally accepted that Canada has been a territory of a monarch or a monarchy in its own right only since the establishment of the French [colony of Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") in the early 16th century;[[41]](#cite_note-MacLeod6-41) according to historian [Jacques Monet](/wiki/Jacques_Monet "Jacques Monet"), the Canadian Crown is one of the few that have survived through uninterrupted succession since before its inception.[[45]](#cite_note-Monet-46)

After the Canadian colonies of France were, via war and treaties, ceded to the British Crown, and the population was greatly expanded by [those loyal to George III](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalists "United Empire Loyalists") fleeing north from persecution during and following the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") was in 1867 [confederated](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada") by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") to form Canada as a kingdom in its own right.[[69]](#cite_note-DCHCrown-75)[[216]](#cite_note-Buck1-233)[[509]](#cite_note-537) By the end of the First World War, the increased fortitude of [Canadian nationalism](/wiki/Canadian_nationalism "Canadian nationalism") inspired the country's leaders to push for greater independence from the King in his British Council, resulting in the creation of the uniquely Canadian monarchy through the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), which was granted royal assent in 1931.[[63]](#cite_note-MacLeod9-68)[[510]](#cite_note-538) Only five years later, Canada had three successive kings in the space of one year, with the death of [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), the [accession and abdication](/wiki/Edward_VIII_abdication_crisis "Edward VIII abdication crisis") of [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII"), and his replacement by [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI").

From 1786 through to the 1930s, members of the royal family toured Canada, including [Prince William](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") (later King William IV); [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent"); [Prince Albert Edward, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") (later King Edward VII); [Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn"); [John Campbell, Marquess of Lorne](/wiki/John_Campbell,_9th_Duke_of_Argyll "John Campbell, 9th Duke of Argyll"), and [Princess Louise](/wiki/Princess_Louise,_Duchess_of_Argyll "Princess Louise, Duchess of Argyll"); [Prince Leopold](/wiki/Prince_Leopold,_Duke_of_Albany "Prince Leopold, Duke of Albany"); [Princess Marie-Louise](/wiki/Princess_Marie_Louise_of_Schleswig-Holstein "Princess Marie Louise of Schleswig-Holstein"); Prince George, Duke of Cornwall and York (later King George V), and [Princess Victoria](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") (later Queen Mary); [Prince Arthur](/wiki/Prince_Arthur_of_Connaught "Prince Arthur of Connaught") (son of the Duke of Connaught); [Princess Patricia](/wiki/Princess_Patricia_of_Connaught "Princess Patricia of Connaught"); Prince Albert (later King George VI); Prince Edward, Prince of Wales (later King Edward VIII); [Prince George, Duke of Kent](/wiki/Prince_George,_Duke_of_Kent "Prince George, Duke of Kent"); and [Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Prince Henry, Duke of Gloucester").[[511]](#cite_note-539)

### The Canadian Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: The Canadian Crown")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/George_VI_visits_Woodbine_Race_Track.jpg/250px-George_VI_visits_Woodbine_Race_Track.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_VI_visits_Woodbine_Race_Track.jpg)

King George VI and Queen Elizabeth attending the [King's Plate](/wiki/King%27s_Plate "King's Plate") in Toronto during their 1939 royal tour

King George VI became in 1939 the first reigning monarch of Canada to [tour the country](/wiki/1939_royal_tour_of_Canada "1939 royal tour of Canada"), doing so with his wife, [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother"). Only weeks later, the King, on the advice of his Canadian Prime Minister, [declared war on Nazi Germany](/wiki/Canadian_declaration_of_war_on_Germany "Canadian declaration of war on Germany").[[512]](#cite_note-540) Throughout [the conflict](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), George boosted the [morale](/wiki/Morale "Morale") of his Canadian troops[[513]](#cite_note-541) while Governor General [the Earl of Athlone](/wiki/Alexander_Cambridge,_1st_Earl_of_Athlone "Alexander Cambridge, 1st Earl of Athlone") (the King's uncle) supported the war effort in Canada. The men were occasionally assisted in their efforts by other members of the royal family.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_and_Prince_Philip_sit_on_thrones_before_a_full_Parliament.jpg)

Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), wearing her [coronation gown](/wiki/Coronation_gown_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation gown of Elizabeth II"), with [Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") during the opening of the [23rd Canadian Parliament](/wiki/23rd_Canadian_Parliament "23rd Canadian Parliament"), October 1957

[Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") undertook her first tour of Canada in 1951, when Princess Elizabeth, Duchess of Edinburgh. She would go on to officiate at various moments of importance in the nation's history: She opened Parliament in 1957[[514]](#cite_note-542)—on the same tour, delivering, from Rideau Hall, her first-ever [live television](/wiki/Live_television "Live television") broadcast[[515]](#cite_note-543)—and in 1977; opened the [St. Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Seaway "St. Lawrence Seaway") in 1959;[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) celebrated the [Canadian Centennial](/wiki/Canadian_Centennial "Canadian Centennial");[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) and proclaimed the country to be fully independent, via constitutional [patriation](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation"), in 1982.[[456]](#cite_note-MacLeod11-480) That act is said to have entrenched the monarchy in Canada,[[516]](#cite_note-544) due to the stringent amending formula that must be followed in order to alter the monarchy in any way.[[170]](#cite_note-ElizabethII1982-183)

Through the 1960s and 1970s, the rise of [Quebec nationalism](/wiki/Quebec_nationalism "Quebec nationalism") and changes in [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity") created an atmosphere where the purpose and role of the monarchy [came into question](/wiki/Debate_on_the_monarchy_in_Canada "Debate on the monarchy in Canada"). Some references to the monarch and the monarchy were removed from the public eye and moves were made by the federal government to constitutionally alter the Crown's place and role in Canada, first by explicit legal amendments and later by subtle attrition.[[240]](#cite_note-GardnerRight-257)[[517]](#cite_note-545)[[518]](#cite_note-Tidridge19-546) But, provincial and federal ministers, along with loyal [national citizen's organizations](/wiki/Monarchism_in_Canada "Monarchism in Canada"), ensured that the system remained the same in essence.[[519]](#cite_note-547)

The Queen publicly expressed her personal support for the [Meech Lake Accord](/wiki/Meech_Lake_Accord "Meech Lake Accord"), which attempted to bring Quebec governmental support to the patriated constitution.[[520]](#cite_note-548) The accord failed, prompting Elizabeth to deliver a nationally-broadcast speech in Ottawa supporting Canadian unity.[[521]](#cite_note-549) In the lead-up to the [referendum on Quebec independence](/wiki/1995_Quebec_independence_referendum "1995 Quebec independence referendum") in 1995, the Queen was tricked by [a Montreal radio DJ](/wiki/Pierre_Brassard "Pierre Brassard") into revealing her desire to see the "no" side win, offering to help however she could.[[522]](#cite_note-550)[[523]](#cite_note-BousfieldMC-551) Elizabeth followed the results closely on the day of the vote.[[523]](#cite_note-BousfieldMC-551)

Members of the royal family continued to be present at important national events through the decades: the Queen in 1970, 1971, and 1973, respectively, marked the anniversaries of [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), and [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island") becoming Canadian provinces; celebrated [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")'s and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick")'s bicentennials in 1984 and the 125th anniversary of Confederation in 1992; and she opened the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) and [Nunavut's parliament](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Nunavut "Legislative Assembly of Nunavut") in 1999. [Prince Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), attended the 100th anniversary of [Treaty 7](/wiki/Treaty_7 "Treaty 7") in 1977; commemorated in 1983 the bicentennial of [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalists "United Empire Loyalists") settling in New Brunswick and [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"); and, with [Diana, Princess of Wales](/wiki/Diana,_Princess_of_Wales "Diana, Princess of Wales"), opened [Expo 86](/wiki/Expo_86 "Expo 86") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"). Between them, the Queen and her family opened numerous [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), [Commonwealth Heads of Government Meetings](/wiki/Commonwealth_Heads_of_Government_Meeting "Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting"), conferences, hospitals, community centres, and the like; handed out [The Duke of Edinburgh Awards](/wiki/The_Duke_of_Edinburgh_Award "The Duke of Edinburgh Award") at ceremonies across the country, and visited many regiments and branches of the [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces").[[524]](#cite_note-552)

### The 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: The 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5f/Official_Diamond_Jubilee_Portrait_of_the_Queen_of_Canada.jpg/250px-Official_Diamond_Jubilee_Portrait_of_the_Queen_of_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_Diamond_Jubilee_Portrait_of_the_Queen_of_Canada.jpg)

Elizabeth II, the first monarch to be titled *Queen of Canada*, wearing her Canadian insignia, as sovereign of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") and the [Order of Military Merit](/wiki/Order_of_Military_Merit_(Canada) "Order of Military Merit (Canada)"), 2010

By 2002, the royal tour and associated [fêtes](/wiki/F%C3%AAte "Fête") for [the Queen's Golden Jubilee](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II#Canada "Golden Jubilee of Elizabeth II") proved popular with Canadians across the country,[[525]](#cite_note-553)[[526]](#cite_note-554)[[527]](#cite_note-555) though Canada's first republican organization since the 1830s was also founded that year. Celebrations took place across the country to mark [the Queen's Diamond Jubilee](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II") in 2012,[[528]](#cite_note-556)[[529]](#cite_note-557) the first such event in Canada since that for Victoria in 1897. On 9 September 2015, she became the second-longest reigning monarch in Canadian history (preceded only by King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"));[[530]](#cite_note-558) events were organized to celebrate her as the "longest-reigning sovereign in Canada's modern era."[[531]](#cite_note-559) [Prince Charles](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") represented his mother, the Queen, two years later, at the main events in Ottawa recognizing the [150th anniversary of Confederation](/wiki/150th_anniversary_of_Canada "150th anniversary of Canada").[[532]](#cite_note-560)

During the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the Queen expressed her support for all Canadians and thanks to those who were caring for the vulnerable and providing essential services.[[533]](#cite_note-561) As the pandemic waned into 2022, celebrations were mounted around the country and throughout the year to mark [the Queen's Platinum Jubilee](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II#Canada "Platinum Jubilee of Elizabeth II");[[534]](#cite_note-562) the first-ever such event in Canadian history.[[535]](#cite_note-563) It was also, though, the first time since at least [Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Queen_Victoria "Golden Jubilee of Queen Victoria") in 1887 that the federal Cabinet did not advise the Crown to create an associated medal.[[536]](#cite_note-564) In response, six provinces produced their own [Platinum Jubilee medals](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Platinum_Jubilee_Medal "Queen Elizabeth II Platinum Jubilee Medal"); another first.[[537]](#cite_note-565)

The subject of [reconciliation with Canada's Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Truth_and_Reconciliation_Commission_of_Canada "Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada") came to the forefront of the public consciousness in 2021, particularly in regard to [residential schools](/wiki/Canadian_Indian_residential_school_system "Canadian Indian residential school system"). Statues of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Statue_of_Queen_Victoria_(Winnipeg) "Statue of Queen Victoria (Winnipeg)") and [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Statue_of_Elizabeth_II_(Winnipeg) "Statue of Elizabeth II (Winnipeg)") in [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg") were vandalized.[[538]](#cite_note-Bergen-566)[[539]](#cite_note-567) On the first [National Day for Truth and Reconciliation](/wiki/National_Day_for_Truth_and_Reconciliation "National Day for Truth and Reconciliation"), Elizabeth made a public statement, saying she "joins with all Canadians [...] to reflect on the painful history that Indigenous peoples endured in residential schools in Canada and on the work that remains to heal and to continue to build an inclusive society."[[540]](#cite_note-568) In the same year, the Queen appointed [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon") as the first Indigenous governor general in Canadian history.[[n 29]](#cite_note-LGs-569)[[541]](#cite_note-570) During Charles's tour for his mother's Platinum Jubilee, the BBC's royal correspondent observed that "there [was] no shying away from acknowledging and highlighting the scandalous way many indigenous peoples have been treated in Canada."[[320]](#cite_note-Campbell-338)

[Queen Elizabeth II died on 8 September 2022](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Elizabeth_II "Death and state funeral of Elizabeth II") and was succeeded by her eldest son, [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"). The Queen's final public statement was issued on 7 September, in the aftermath of the [2022 Saskatchewan stabbings](/wiki/2022_Saskatchewan_stabbings "2022 Saskatchewan stabbings"),[[542]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorrance2022b-571)[[543]](#cite_note-572) stating she "mourn[s] with all Canadians at this tragic time."[[544]](#cite_note-573) Elizabeth reigned for almost half of Canada's history since Confederation,[[545]](#cite_note-574) being only the sixth Canadian monarch since 1867.[[546]](#cite_note-575)

> The Crown has for so long been a symbol of unity for Canada. It also represents stability and continuity from the past to the present. As it should, it stands proudly as a symbol of Canada today, in all her richness and dynamism.[[547]](#cite_note-576)

Charles III, [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/2025_Speech_from_the_Throne "2025 Speech from the Throne"), 2025

Immediately following a formal meeting of the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada"), the [new king was proclaimed](/wiki/Proclamation_of_accession_of_Charles_III#Canada "Proclamation of accession of Charles III") on 10 September in a ceremony at [Rideau Hall](/wiki/Rideau_Hall "Rideau Hall").[[548]](#cite_note-577)[[549]](#cite_note-578) On 4 May 2023, the King held audiences with Simon and Indigenous leaders, who also attended [his coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") two days later.[[550]](#cite_note-579)
