In May 2025, King Charles III, accompanied by [Queen Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla"), visited Canada for the first time as monarch and delivered the [speech from the throne](/wiki/2025_Speech_from_the_Throne "2025 Speech from the Throne"), the first time a Canadian monarch has done so in person since 1977.[[551]](#cite_note-580)

## Public perception and understanding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Public perception and understanding")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c1/His_Majesty_King_Charles_III%2C_King_of_Canada.jpg/250px-His_Majesty_King_Charles_III%2C_King_of_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:His_Majesty_King_Charles_III,_King_of_Canada.jpg)

King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") delivering the [2025 Speech from the Throne](/wiki/2025_Speech_from_the_Throne "2025 Speech from the Throne") in the [Senate of Canada Building](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada_Building "Senate of Canada Building"), May 2025

Prior to the 1970s, Canadians' view of the monarchy was more focused on the person of the monarch than the institution's place in the country's framework.[[475]](#cite_note-Heard115-501) Smith, in 2017, and Jackson, in 2018, observed the shift continuing, especially as "the process of 'Canadianization' of the Crown picked up momentum in the early 21st century."[[552]](#cite_note-581)[[553]](#cite_note-Jackson15-582)

Still, beginning in the later decades of the 20th century, commentators stated that contemporary Canadians had and have a poor understanding of the Canadian monarchy;[[554]](#cite_note-583) something the Monarchist League of Canada (MLC) claims opponents of the monarchy exacerbate by spreading disinformation and then take advantage of.[[555]](#cite_note-myths-584) Jackson wrote in his book, *The Crown and Canadian Federalism*, that this is part of a wider ignorance about Canadian civics[[556]](#cite_note-Jackson11-585) and Hugo Cyr agreed,[[557]](#cite_note-586) while Smith researched for his 1995 book, *The Invisible Crown*, he found it difficult to "find anyone who could talk knowledgeably about the subject".[[558]](#cite_note-Treble-587) Former Governors General Clarkson and Johnston made similar observations[[164]](#cite_note-Johnstonxi-175)[[559]](#cite_note-588) and Senator [Lowell Murray](/wiki/Lowell_Murray "Lowell Murray") wrote in 2003, "the Crown has become irrelevant to most Canadians' understanding of our system of government", which he attributed to the "fault of successive generations of politicians, of an educational system that has never given the institution due study, and of past viceregal incumbents themselves".[[560]](#cite_note-589)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/1_panorama_lake_patricia_2006_alberta.jpg/250px-1_panorama_lake_patricia_2006_alberta.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_panorama_lake_patricia_2006_alberta.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Queen_TTC_title_tiles_6756443839.jpg/250px-Queen_TTC_title_tiles_6756443839.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_TTC_title_tiles_6756443839.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Royal_Newfoundland_Constabulary_flash.png/120px-Royal_Newfoundland_Constabulary_flash.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Newfoundland_Constabulary_flash.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/1996_Ontario_license_plate_149%E2%99%94OVH_commercial.jpg/250px-1996_Ontario_license_plate_149%E2%99%94OVH_commercial.jpg)](/wiki/File:1996_Ontario_license_plate_149%E2%99%94OVH_commercial.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/A_river_runs_through_it_%2844196184722%29.jpg/250px-A_river_runs_through_it_%2844196184722%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_river_runs_through_it_(44196184722).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Burgee_of_Royal_Vancouver_YC.svg/250px-Burgee_of_Royal_Vancouver_YC.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Burgee_of_Royal_Vancouver_YC.svg)

Examples of the prevalence of royal references and images around Canada (from top): [Patricia Lake](/wiki/Patricia_Lake_(Alberta) "Patricia Lake (Alberta)"), named for [Princess Patricia of Connaught](/wiki/Princess_Patricia_of_Connaught "Princess Patricia of Connaught"); [Queen station](/wiki/Queen_station "Queen station") beneath [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")'s [Queen Street](/wiki/Queen_Street,_Toronto "Queen Street, Toronto"), so named for [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"); badge of the [Royal Newfoundland Constabulary](/wiki/Royal_Newfoundland_Constabulary "Royal Newfoundland Constabulary"); [Ontario vehicle licence plate](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ontario "Vehicle registration plates of Ontario") showing the silhouette of a crown; [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), named for [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn"); [burgee](/wiki/Burgee "Burgee") of the [Royal Vancouver Yacht Club](/wiki/Royal_Vancouver_Yacht_Club "Royal Vancouver Yacht Club")

On education, teacher and author Nathan Tidridge asserted that, beginning in the 1960s, the role of the Crown disappeared from provincial education curricula, as the general subject of civics came to receive less attention.[[518]](#cite_note-Tidridge19-546) He said Canadians are being "educated to be illiterate, ambivalent, or even hostile toward our constitutional monarchy".[[556]](#cite_note-Jackson11-585) The MLC agreed, stating Canada has "an educational system which unfortunately often fails to provide comprehensive knowledge of Canada's constitution."[[555]](#cite_note-myths-584)

[Michael Valpy](/wiki/Michael_Valpy "Michael Valpy") also pointed to the fact that "the Crown's role in the machinery of Canada's constitutional monarchy rarely sees daylight. Only a handful of times in our history has it been subjected to glaring sunshine, unfortunately resulting in a black hole of public understanding as to how it works."[[561]](#cite_note-590) He later iterated, "the public's attention span on the constitutional intricacies of the monarchy is clinically short".[[562]](#cite_note-591) At the same time, it has been theorized the monarchy is so prevalent in Canada—by way of all manner of symbols, place names, royal tours, etcetera—that Canadians fail to take note of it; the monarchy "functions like a tasteful wallpaper pattern in Canada: enjoyable in an absent-minded way, but so ubiquitous as to be almost invisible".[[445]](#cite_note-Proudfoot-468)

John Pepall argued in 1990 that, among all this, a "Liberal-inspired republican misconception of the role" of governor general had taken root, though the Conservative government headed by [Brian Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney") exacerbated the matter.[[563]](#cite_note-Pepall-592) The position of prime minister has simultaneously undergone, with encouragement from its occupants,[[563]](#cite_note-Pepall-592) what has been described as a "presidentialization",[[564]](#cite_note-593)[[565]](#cite_note-594) to the point that its incumbents publicly outshine the actual head of state.[[566]](#cite_note-595)[[567]](#cite_note-596) David S. Donovan felt Canadians mostly consider the monarch and her representatives as purely ceremonial and symbolic figures,[[568]](#cite_note-597) while also still viewing the sovereign as British, even if they understand he is King of Canada.[[474]](#cite_note-Heard126-500) It was argued by Alfred Neitsch that this undermined the Crown's legitimacy as a check and balance in the governmental system,[[569]](#cite_note-598) a situation Helen Forsey (daughter of Canadian constitutional expert [Eugene Forsey](/wiki/Eugene_Forsey "Eugene Forsey")) said prime ministers take advantage of, portraying themselves as the embodiment of popular democracy and the reserve powers of the Crown as illegitimate. The issue is particularly acute in Quebec,[[265]](#cite_note-Cyr-282) where sovereigntist politicians consider the Canadian monarchy as British, foreign, and, consequently, as symbol of oppression.[[570]](#cite_note-Serebrin-599)

Beginning slightly earlier, a "growing interest in the Crown and its prerogatives" was observed, as evidenced by "a burst of articles, books and conferences";[[558]](#cite_note-Treble-587) the monarchy attracted increased attention from academics, as well as those involved with the law, government, and public policy.[[571]](#cite_note-600) This was attributed to the coincidental occurrence of publicly prominent events over a number of years, including the [2008 prorogation dispute](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Canadian_parliamentary_dispute "2008–09 Canadian parliamentary dispute"); an increased use of royal symbols as directed by the Cabinet while headed by Stephen Harper, including three consecutive royal tours;[[572]](#cite_note-601) court cases focusing on the Oath of Citizenship; and increasingly active governors. Smith and Lagassé noted in early 2016 that post-secondary students were giving more focus to the subject of the Crown.[[558]](#cite_note-Treble-587)

Some Canadians have taken the opposite extreme view of the Crown's powers, such as when, in 2013, the leader of the [Green Party of Canada](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Canada "Green Party of Canada"), [Elizabeth May](/wiki/Elizabeth_May "Elizabeth May"), wrote to Queen Elizabeth II asking her to call a "[royal inquiry](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_royal_commissions "List of Canadian royal commissions")" to "investigate what may potentially be criminal activities which [sic] influenced Canada's last election" and "restore Canada to a free and fair democracy." The monarch's secretary instructed May, "as a constitutional sovereign, Her Majesty acts through her personal representative, the Governor-General, on the advice of her Canadian ministers and it is to them that your appeal should be directed."[[573]](#cite_note-602) So many protesters involved in the 2022 [Freedom Convoy](/wiki/Canada_convoy_protest "Canada convoy protest") called and emailed the office of Governor General Mary Simon to pressure her into dismissing her Prime Minister, Justin Trudeau, or dissolving Parliament that Simon made a rare public statement on the role of the governor general. Lagassé pointed to that as "evidence of a recent trend in protest movements", saying, "it has become routine in Canadian politics to write a letter to the Queen, Governor General, or a lieutenant governor asking them to exercise their powers in some way, contrary to constitutional conventions. This is political theatre, no more."[[574]](#cite_note-603)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Queen_Victoria_in_front_of_the_Manitoba_Legislative_Assembly_%E2%80%93_Winnipeg_%2844011134692%29.jpg/250px-Queen_Victoria_in_front_of_the_Manitoba_Legislative_Assembly_%E2%80%93_Winnipeg_%2844011134692%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Victoria_in_front_of_the_Manitoba_Legislative_Assembly_%E2%80%93_Winnipeg_(44011134692).jpg)

The [statue of Queen Victoria](/wiki/Statue_of_Queen_Victoria_(Winnipeg) "Statue of Queen Victoria (Winnipeg)") at the [Manitoba Legislative Building](/wiki/Manitoba_Legislative_Building "Manitoba Legislative Building") that was destroyed by protesters on [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day"), 2021

The relationship between Canada's Indigenous peoples and the monarch remains unchanged, aside from the issue of [reconciliation](/wiki/Reconciliation_(ethnic_relations) "Reconciliation (ethnic relations)") arising in it through the late 2010s into the 2020s,[[553]](#cite_note-Jackson15-582) when there were some assertions by activists and in the media that the monarchy and the Queen herself represented colonialism[[575]](#cite_note-604)[[576]](#cite_note-605)[[577]](#cite_note-Iqbal-606)[[578]](#cite_note-607) and racism[[579]](#cite_note-608) and she did not do enough to either prevent or rectify supposed offences.[[577]](#cite_note-Iqbal-606) Those who made such claims also, though, mistook the independent Canadian Crown[[580]](#cite_note-609) as the British Crown in Canada and demonstrated a misunderstanding of the Crown-Indigenous peoples relationship[[581]](#cite_note-Tidridge2020-610)[[582]](#cite_note-611) and the workings of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"),[[583]](#cite_note-612) in which the sovereign must, outside of [constitutional crises](/wiki/Constitutional_crisis "Constitutional crisis"), follow [the directions](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") of their ministers and parliamentarians.[[581]](#cite_note-Tidridge2020-610)[[584]](#cite_note-613)[[585]](#cite_note-614)[[586]](#cite_note-615) The leader of the [Parti Québécois](/wiki/Parti_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Parti Québécois"), [Paul St-Pierre Plamondon](/wiki/Paul_St-Pierre_Plamondon "Paul St-Pierre Plamondon"), claimed in 2022, "we cannot overlook that she [Queen Elizabeth II] represented an institution, the British Crown, that has caused significant harm to Quebecers and Indigenous nations."[[570]](#cite_note-Serebrin-599)

Within Quebec, too, the Canadian Crown is often depicted as being synonymous with the British Crown, with [Quebec sovereigntists](/wiki/Quebec_sovereignty_movement "Quebec sovereignty movement") arguing that it is fundamentally foreign to Quebec and should be abolished within the province as a part of Quebec independence. For instance, in the televised [Radio-Canada](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") leaders' debate on 22 September 2022, during [that year's general election in the province](/wiki/2022_Quebec_general_election "2022 Quebec general election"), the moderator, [Patrice Roy](/wiki/Patrice_Roy "Patrice Roy"), asked the panel, with "incredulous chuckles", "should we still, in Quebec, swear allegiance to the British Crown, thus Charles III [to take one's seat in the National Assembly]?"[[587]](#cite_note-616) [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois") leader [Yves-François Blanchet](/wiki/Yves-Fran%C3%A7ois_Blanchet "Yves-François Blanchet") on 26 October 2022 tabled a motion in the [House of Commons](/wiki/Canadian_House_of_Commons "Canadian House of Commons") proposing that the "House express its desire to sever ties between the Canadian state and the British monarchy."[[588]](#cite_note-parl-617) This motion was defeated 266 to 44.[[588]](#cite_note-parl-617)

## Debate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Debate")]

Main article: [Debate on the monarchy in Canada](/wiki/Debate_on_the_monarchy_in_Canada "Debate on the monarchy in Canada")

Further information: [Monarchism in Canada](/wiki/Monarchism_in_Canada "Monarchism in Canada") and [Republicanism in Canada](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Canada "Republicanism in Canada")

Outside of academic circles, there has been little national debate on the monarchy.[[589]](#cite_note-618) The position of monarch in Canada is highly protected by the *[Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982")*—which mandates that any major constitutional amendment, such as any change to the monarchy, must receive the consent of the Senate, the House of Commons, and all ten provincial legislative assemblies. Abolishing the monarchy may also be complicated by the fact that First Nations treaties are officially between the Crown and [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada").[[n 30]](#cite_note-whathappened-620)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/54/Wessex-MLC.jpg/250px-Wessex-MLC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wessex-MLC.jpg)

[Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Earl_of_Wessex "Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex"), speaks with members of the [Monarchist League of Canada](/wiki/Monarchist_League_of_Canada "Monarchist League of Canada") at a League reception held at the Spoke Club in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")

Canada has two special-interest groups representing the debate, who occasionally argue the issue in the media: the [Monarchist League of Canada](/wiki/Monarchist_League_of_Canada "Monarchist League of Canada") (MLC) and [Citizens for a Canadian Republic](/wiki/Citizens_for_a_Canadian_Republic "Citizens for a Canadian Republic").[[591]](#cite_note-621)[[592]](#cite_note-622) There are also other organizations that support and advocate the monarchy, such as the Institute for the Study of the Crown in Canada,[[593]](#cite_note-623) the Canadian Royal Heritage Trust,[[594]](#cite_note-624) the Friends of the Canadian Crown,[[595]](#cite_note-625) Canadian Friends of the Royal Family,[[596]](#cite_note-626) the [Société de la Couronne du Canada](/w/index.php?title=Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_la_Couronne_du_Canada&action=edit&redlink=1 "Société de la Couronne du Canada (page does not exist)")[[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_la_Couronne_du_Canada "fr:Société de la Couronne du Canada")],[[597]](#cite_note-627) the [Orange Order in Canada](/wiki/Orange_Order_in_Canada "Orange Order in Canada"),[[598]](#cite_note-628) and the [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist")' Association of Canada.[[599]](#cite_note-629)

Out of Canada's four most prominent political parties, neither the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") nor the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") are officially in favour of abolishing the monarchy (the Conservative Party cite support for constitutional monarchy as a founding principle in its policy declaration)[[600]](#cite_note-630) and the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") has no official position on the role of the Crown. Only some individual members of Parliament belonging to these parties and the leaders of the [Bloc Québécois](/wiki/Bloc_Qu%C3%A9b%C3%A9cois "Bloc Québécois") party have made any statements suggesting abolition of the monarchy.[[601]](#cite_note-631)[[602]](#cite_note-632)

[Opinion polls on the Canadian monarchy](/wiki/Debate_on_the_monarchy_in_Canada#Polls "Debate on the monarchy in Canada") have been regularly conducted since the 1990s. An analysis of these polls in 2008 highlighted an increased disaffection with the monarchy, albeit with internal contradictions in specific polling results, with some criticizing the polling questions for using "inconsistent and sometimes ambiguous wording."[[603]](#cite_note-633) Questions often describe the monarch or monarchy as "British", terminology at odds with the contemporary situation in Canada, wherein the monarchy is a Canadian institution,[[604]](#cite_note-634)[[45]](#cite_note-Monet-46)[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40) separate from that of the United Kingdom,[[65]](#cite_note-Trepanier28-70)[[68]](#cite_note-HCA-73)[[71]](#cite_note-Buchan1969-77)[[315]](#cite_note-DCHsymb-333)[[605]](#cite_note-635) and it, the Crown, and royal family are referred to as Canadian.[[35]](#cite_note-Harper-35)[[39]](#cite_note-Kenney-39)[[438]](#cite_note-SaskProtocol-460)[[606]](#cite_note-636)[[607]](#cite_note-637)[[608]](#cite_note-638)[[609]](#cite_note-639)[[610]](#cite_note-640) Both monarchists and republicans agree the populace's general lack of understanding about the monarchy affects opinions.[[555]](#cite_note-myths-584)[[611]](#cite_note-CCR-641)

The idea of a uniquely Canadian monarch,[[612]](#cite_note-642) either one descended from the House of Windsor or coming from a First Nations [royal house](/wiki/Royal_house "Royal house"),[[613]](#cite_note-643)[[614]](#cite_note-644)[[615]](#cite_note-645)[[616]](#cite_note-646) has been proffered as an alternative. However, there has been no popular or official support for such a change.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** The date of the first establishment of monarchy in Canada varies: some sources give the year as 1497, when [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot") landed somewhere along the North American coast (most likely Nova Scotia or Newfoundland) claiming an undefined extent of land for King [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"),[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Harper-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) while others put it at 1534, when the [colony of Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") was founded in the name of King [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France").[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-DCHMon-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Kenney-39)[[40]](#cite_note-Valpy1109-40)[[41]](#cite_note-MacLeod6-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Senate-42) Historian Carolyn Harris places the beginning of Canada's monarchical government at the appointment of [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") as [Governor of New France](/wiki/Governor_of_New_France "Governor of New France"), representing King [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII"), in 1627.[[43]](#cite_note-43)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Maple_53-0)** The term was first coined by [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") [the Lord Grey](/wiki/Albert_Grey,_4th_Earl_Grey "Albert Grey, 4th Earl Grey") in 1905, when he stated in a [telegram](/wiki/Telegram "Telegram") to King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") regarding the inauguration of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan") into [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation"): "[each one] a new leaf in Your Majesty's Maple Crown".[[39]](#cite_note-Kenney-39)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-ViceDuties_61-0)** For instance, Governor General [Jules Léger](/wiki/Jules_L%C3%A9ger "Jules Léger") ended the second session of the [30th Canadian Parliament](/wiki/30th_Canadian_Parliament "30th Canadian Parliament") on 17 October 1977, while Queen Elizabeth II was in Ottawa to deliver the Speech from the Throne at the opening of the third session on 18 October. Governor General [Adrienne Clarkson](/wiki/Adrienne_Clarkson "Adrienne Clarkson") granted royal assent to four bills on 19 May 2005,[[58]](#cite_note-60) while Queen Elizabeth II was touring Saskatchewan and Alberta to mark the 100th anniversary of those provinces joining Confederation.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-RoyVis_62-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-RoyVis_62-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-RoyVis_62-2) The sovereign has [carried out foreign relations as the representative uniquely of Canada](#Foreign_visits).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-RoyDuties_66-0)** For example, Queen Elizabeth II was in the United Kingdom when she signed the [proclamation of the National Flag of Canada](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada#Proclamation "Flag of Canada") in 1965.[[59]](#cite_note-63) King George VI was in the United Kingdom when, as king of Canada, he [declared war on Germany](/wiki/Canadian_declaration_of_war_on_Germany "Canadian declaration of war on Germany") in 1939.[[60]](#cite_note-64)[[61]](#cite_note-65)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-ECApp_74-0)** The [English Court of Appeal](/wiki/English_Court_of_Appeal "English Court of Appeal") ruled in 1982, while "there is only one person who is the Sovereign within the British Commonwealth [...] in matters of law and government the Queen of the United Kingdom, for example, is entirely independent and distinct from the Queen of Canada."[[68]](#cite_note-HCA-73)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-Black_79-0)** In 1997, UK Prime Minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") intended to offer a [life peerage](/wiki/Life_peer "Life peer") to Canadian businessman [Conrad Black](/wiki/Conrad_Black "Conrad Black"). However, citing the 1919 [Nickle Resolution](/wiki/Nickle_Resolution "Nickle Resolution"), the Canadian Cabinet advised the Queen not to grant Black such an honour. If Blair had not yielded, the Queen would have been in the situation of having to grant an honour on the advice of her British Prime Minister and to object to the same as Queen of Canada on the advice of Canadian Prime Minister [Jean Chrétien](/wiki/Jean_Chr%C3%A9tien "Jean Chrétien").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-EdVIII1_109-0)** For example, [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") was never crowned, yet was undoubtedly king during his short time on the throne.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-EdVIII2_113-0)** The only Canadian monarch to abdicate, Edward VIII, did so with the authorization of the Canadian government granted in *[His Majesty's Declaration of Abdication Act, 1936](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Declaration_of_Abdication_Act_1936 "His Majesty's Declaration of Abdication Act 1936")*, and the *[Succession to the Throne Act, 1937](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Throne_Act,_1937 "Succession to the Throne Act, 1937")*, later confirmed this in law.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-Marriages_122-0)** Gary Toffoli of the Canadian Royal Heritage Trust stated that the approval given by the Queen in her Canadian Council in [1981 to the marriage](/wiki/Wedding_of_Prince_Charles_and_Lady_Diana_Spencer "Wedding of Prince Charles and Lady Diana Spencer") of [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), and [Lady Diana Spencer](/wiki/Diana,_Princess_of_Wales "Diana, Princess of Wales")[[111]](#cite_note-Boyce81-120) separately to the same approval given by the Queen in her British Council illustrated the existence of the *Royal Marriages Act* in Canadian law.[[112]](#cite_note-121) In 1947, the King in his Canadian Council gave the same consent to the marriage of Princess Elizabeth to Philip Mountbatten, again separate from the approval he gave in his British Council.[[111]](#cite_note-Boyce81-120)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-Sovereignty_171-0)** Upon acceding to the throne on 8 September 2022, King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") stated, "I am deeply aware of this great inheritance and of the duties and heavy responsibilities of sovereignty, which have now passed to me. In taking up these responsibilities, I shall strive to follow the inspiring example I have been set in upholding constitutional government and to seek the peace, harmony, and prosperity of [...] the Commonwealth realms and territories throughout the world."[[160]](#cite_note-170)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-Pers2_180-0)** For example, if a lawsuit is filed against the federal government, the respondent is formally described as *His Majesty the King in Right of Canada*, or simply *[Rex](/wiki/Rex_(title) "Rex (title)")*.[[134]](#cite_note-CRHT2-144) Likewise, in a case in which a party sues both the province of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan") and the federal government, the respondents would be formally called *His Majesty the King in Right of Saskatchewan* and *His Majesty the King in Right of Canada*.[[168]](#cite_note-179)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-OMNR_182-0)** Illustrative of this arrangement is property transfers; of this, the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources states: "When public land is required by the federal government or one of its departments, or any provincial ministry, the land itself is not transferred. What is transferred is the responsibility to manage the lands on behalf of Her Majesty the Queen (HMQ). This is accomplished by an order-in-council or a minister's order, which transfers management of land either from HMQ in right of Ontario to HMQ in right of Canada as represented by a department or to HMQ in right of Ontario as represented by another ministry. The Crown does not transfer ownership to itself."[[169]](#cite_note-181)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-REH_188-0)** Robert E. Hawkins called this "inefficient efficiency", which can "be placed alongside the other oxymorons that convention embeds in our constitution, oxymorons like loyal opposition, liberal democracy, and constitutional monarchy itself."[[174]](#cite_note-Hawkins104-187)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-Pers1_210-0)** The sovereign has been described by [Eugene Forsey](/wiki/Eugene_Forsey "Eugene Forsey") as the "symbolic embodiment of the people—not a particular group or interest or party, but the people, the whole people";[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88) his daughter, Helen Forsey, said of his opinion on the Crown, "for him, the essence of the monarchy was its impartial representation of the common interests of the citizenry as a whole, as opposed to those of any particular government."[[81]](#cite_note-Forsey-88) The [Department of Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Department_of_Canadian_Heritage "Department of Canadian Heritage") said the Crown serves as the "personal symbol of allegiance, unity and authority for all Canadians,"[[69]](#cite_note-DCHCrown-75)[[192]](#cite_note-DCH3-206) a concept akin to that expressed by King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France"): "*l'État, c'est moi*", or, "I am the state".[[193]](#cite_note-ontla1996-207) [Robertson Davies](/wiki/Robertson_Davies "Robertson Davies") stated in 1994, "the Crown is the consecrated spirit of Canada",[[194]](#cite_note-208) and past Ontario chairman of the [Monarchist League of Canada](/wiki/Monarchist_League_of_Canada "Monarchist League of Canada") Gary Toffoli opined, "the Queen is the legal embodiment of the state at both the national and the provincial levels [...] She is our sovereign and it is the role of the Queen, recognized by the constitutional law of Canada, to embody the state."[[195]](#cite_note-209)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-Boyce_223-0)** As Peter Boyce put it: "The Crown as a concept cannot be disentangled from the person of the monarch; but, standard reference to the Crown extends well beyond the Queen's person."[[207]](#cite_note-222)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-Oath_228-0)** It is stated in the *Rules & Forms of the House of Commons of Canada* that, "allegiance to the King means allegiance to the country."[[211]](#cite_note-227)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-RP_285-0)** See '[Responsibilities](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada#Responsibilities "Cabinet of Canada")' and [Note 1](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada#cite_note-RP-14 "Cabinet of Canada") at [Cabinet of Canada](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada").
19. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Duties_385-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Duties_385-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Duties_385-2) Though the royal family represents other countries abroad, as directed by their respective cabinets, and typically the governor general will undertake [state visits](/wiki/State_visit "State visit") and other foreign duties on behalf of the Queen of Canada,[[198]](#cite_note-Compendium-213)[[351]](#cite_note-369)[[352]](#cite_note-370) members of the royal family will also take part in Canadian events overseas.[[152]](#cite_note-Buckner69-162)[[353]](#cite_note-CRHT-371)[[354]](#cite_note-372)[[355]](#cite_note-373)[[356]](#cite_note-374)[[357]](#cite_note-375)[[358]](#cite_note-376)[[359]](#cite_note-377)[[360]](#cite_note-378)[[361]](#cite_note-379)[[362]](#cite_note-380)[[363]](#cite_note-381)[[364]](#cite_note-382)[[365]](#cite_note-383)[[366]](#cite_note-384)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-Arctic_391-0)** In 1970, Queen Elizabeth II, the Duke of Edinburgh, Prince Charles, and Princess Anne undertook a tour of [Northern Canada](/wiki/Northern_Canada "Northern Canada"), in part to demonstrate to an unconvinced American government and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") that [Canada had certain claim to its Arctic territories](/wiki/Territorial_claims_in_the_Arctic "Territorial claims in the Arctic"), which were strategic during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War").[[371]](#cite_note-390)
21. **[^](#cite_ref-Sharp_399-0)** Former Minister of External Affairs [Mitchell Sharp](/wiki/Mitchell_Sharp "Mitchell Sharp") commented on a situation wherein Elizabeth II was in Latin America to promote British goods at the same time a Canadian ministerial trip to the same area was underway to promote Canadian products. Sharp stated: "We couldn't ask Her Majesty to perform the function she was performing for Britain on that Latin American trip because the Queen is never recognized as Queen of Canada, except when she is in Canada."[[378]](#cite_note-398) The Queen's participation in Canadian events overseas contradicts Sharp's statement, however.[[n 4]](#cite_note-RoyVis-62)[[n 19]](#cite_note-Duties-385)
22. **[^](#cite_ref-CF_406-0)** Such events include [Troopings of the Colour](/wiki/Trooping_the_Colour "Trooping the Colour"), inspections of the troops, and anniversaries of key battles; whenever the sovereign or a member of his family is in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), they lay a wreath at the [National War Memorial](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada) "National War Memorial (Canada)").
23. **[^](#cite_ref-Citizen_465-0)** Two Canadian citizens married into the royal family: In 1988, [Sylvana Jones](/wiki/Sylvana_Windsor,_Countess_of_St_Andrews "Sylvana Windsor, Countess of St Andrews") (née Tomaselli in [Placentia, Newfoundland](/wiki/Placentia,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Placentia, Newfoundland and Labrador")) wed [George Windsor, Earl of St Andrews](/wiki/George_Windsor,_Earl_of_St_Andrews "George Windsor, Earl of St Andrews"), a great-grandson of King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), and, on 18 May 2008, [Autumn Kelly](/wiki/Autumn_Phillips "Autumn Phillips"), originally from [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), married Queen Elizabeth II's eldest grandson, [Peter Phillips](/wiki/Peter_Phillips "Peter Phillips").[[439]](#cite_note-461) The latter couple has two children, 19th and 20th in line to the throne as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit), who each hold dual Canadian and British citizenship,[[440]](#cite_note-462)[[441]](#cite_note-463) as do the three children of George Windsor, though only the youngest, [Lady Amelia Windsor](/wiki/Lady_Amelia_Windsor "Lady Amelia Windsor"), is in line to the throne, 43rd as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Canada&action=edit).[[442]](#cite_note-464) More distantly, Princes Boris and [Hermann Friedrich](/wiki/Prince_Hermann_Friedrich_of_Leiningen "Prince Hermann Friedrich of Leiningen") of Leiningen, great-great-great grandsons of Queen Victoria, are also Canadian citizens.
24. **[^](#cite_ref-CanadianRF_476-0)** As early as 1959, it was recognized that Queen Elizabeth II, was "equally at home in all her realms";[[449]](#cite_note-472) By the 1960s, loyal societies in Canada recognized the Elizabeth's cousin, [Princess Alexandra, the Honourable Lady Ogilvy](/wiki/Princess_Alexandra,_the_Honourable_Lady_Ogilvy "Princess Alexandra, the Honourable Lady Ogilvy"), as a "Canadian princess";[[450]](#cite_note-473) and, at the time of the 2011 royal tour of [Prince William, Duke of Cambridge](/wiki/Prince_William,_Duke_of_Cambridge "Prince William, Duke of Cambridge"), and [Catherine, Duchess of Cambridge](/wiki/Catherine,_Duchess_of_Cambridge "Catherine, Duchess of Cambridge"), both [Michael Valpy](/wiki/Michael_Valpy "Michael Valpy"), writing for the [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation"), and *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")* referred to William as "a prince of Canada".[[451]](#cite_note-474)[[452]](#cite_note-475)
25. **[^](#cite_ref-Canadians_482-0)** In 1919, Prince Edward Albert (the future King [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII")) asserted, "I want Canada to look upon me as a Canadian, if not actually by birth, yet certainly in mind and spirit."[[453]](#cite_note-477) [Princess Elizabeth, Duchess of Edinburgh](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), said in 1951 that, when in Canada, she was "amongst fellow countrymen" and,[[79]](#cite_note-MacLeod3-86)[[454]](#cite_note-478) after acceding to the throne as Queen Elizabeth II, she, when departing the United States for Canada in 1983, stated to [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), "I'm going home to Canada tomorrow".[[455]](#cite_note-479) In 2005, Elizabeth said she agreed with the statement earlier made by her mother, [Queen Elizabeth, the Queen Mother](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother"), that Canada felt like a "home away from home";[[456]](#cite_note-MacLeod11-480) in the same year, she remarked, "I have always felt not only welcome but at home in Canada."[[346]](#cite_note-CHP-364) Similarly, the Queen said in 2010, in Nova Scotia, "it is very good to be home".[[457]](#cite_note-481)
26. **[^](#cite_ref-RF2_488-0)** [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn"), served as the Commander of [British North American](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") troops in Canada's [Maritimes](/wiki/Maritimes "Maritimes") for nine years after 1791, mostly in [Halifax](/wiki/City_of_Halifax "City of Halifax");[[458]](#cite_note-483) his granddaughter, [Princess Louise, Marchioness of Lorne](/wiki/Princess_Louise,_Duchess_of_Argyll "Princess Louise, Duchess of Argyll"), lived in Canada between 1878 and 1883 as [viceregal consort](/wiki/Viceregal_consort_of_Canada "Viceregal consort of Canada");[[459]](#cite_note-484) and her brother, [Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn"), resided in Canada first through 1870 as a member of the [Canadian Militia](/wiki/Canadian_Militia "Canadian Militia"), defending Canada from the [Fenian Raids](/wiki/Fenian_Raids "Fenian Raids"),[[460]](#cite_note-Toffoli-485) and then as governor general from 1911 until 1916.[[461]](#cite_note-486) Later, for six years beginning in 1940, [Princess Alice, Countess of Athlone](/wiki/Princess_Alice,_Countess_of_Athlone "Princess Alice, Countess of Athlone") (a great-grandchild of the Duke of Kent), lived in Canada with her husband, [the Earl of Athlone](/wiki/Alexander_Cambridge,_1st_Earl_of_Athlone "Alexander Cambridge, 1st Earl of Athlone") (himself a great-grandchild of King [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom")),[[460]](#cite_note-Toffoli-485) while he served as governor general.[[462]](#cite_note-GGCam-487)
27. **[^](#cite_ref-Residence_505-0)** While the government houses are the King's official residences in Canada, they are almost exclusively occupied by the sovereign's representative in each of those jurisdictions.[[477]](#cite_note-Lanctot-503)[[478]](#cite_note-504)
28. **[^](#cite_ref-Sussex_531-0)** For example, when [the Duke](/wiki/Prince_Harry,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Harry, Duke of Sussex") and [Duchess of Sussex](/wiki/Meghan,_Duchess_of_Sussex "Meghan, Duchess of Sussex") resided for a time on [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island"), as they were planning their future as members of the royal family, the RCMP provided the couple's security. However, when the Sussexes decided to step down as senior members of the royal family, the RCMP reassessed its provision of service. In a briefing note to the Minister of Public Safety, [Bill Blair](/wiki/Bill_Blair_(politician) "Bill Blair (politician)"), the force noted, "the Sussex family's stay in Canada is of a private nature and, to date, there have been no official outings wherein the Duke and Duchess are representing the Queen. There is no indication of either the Duke or Duchess participating in any official capacity for the Crown in Canada in the next two months. Should this change, however, the RCMP will assess and provide security accordingly."[[503]](#cite_note-530)
29. **[^](#cite_ref-LGs_569-0)** Indigenous persons had already been appointed as lieutenant governors during Elizabeth's reign.
30. **[^](#cite_ref-whathappened_620-0)** Lagassé said, "you would need to have Indigenous peoples involved and consulted properly as well, in particular, since their treaty rights are understood to flow from treaties with the Crown."[[590]](#cite_note-619)
